6.1.1.body:
# Essential Linux Commands for Developers

## Why Linux Matters for Backend Developers

As a backend developer, you will spend a significant portion of your career working with Linux servers. Whether you are deploying applications, debugging production issues, or managing infrastructure, Linux command-line proficiency is non-negotiable. Most cloud servers, Docker containers, and CI/CD environments run Linux.

## The Linux File System Hierarchy

```
/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ bin/        # Essential user binaries (ls, cp, mv)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ etc/        # Configuration files
в”ңв”Җв”Җ home/       # User home directories
в”ңв”Җв”Җ var/        # Variable data (logs, databases)
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ log/    # System and application logs
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ www/    # Web server files
в”ңв”Җв”Җ tmp/        # Temporary files
в”ңв”Җв”Җ usr/        # User programs and data
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ bin/    # Most user commands
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ lib/    # Libraries
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ local/  # Locally installed software
в”ңв”Җв”Җ opt/        # Optional/third-party software
в””в”Җв”Җ proc/       # Virtual filesystem for process info
```

Understanding this hierarchy helps you know where to find configuration files (`/etc`), logs (`/var/log`), and installed software (`/usr/local`).

## Navigation and File Operations

### Moving Around

```bash
# Print current directory
pwd

# Change directory
cd /var/log
cd ~           # Go to home directory
cd -           # Go to previous directory
cd ..          # Go up one level

# List files
ls             # Basic listing
ls -la         # Long format, show hidden files
ls -lah        # Human-readable file sizes
ls -lt         # Sort by modification time
```

### File and Directory Operations

```bash
# Create directories
mkdir myproject
mkdir -p src/controllers/api    # Create nested directories

# Create files
touch server.js
echo "Hello" > greeting.txt     # Create with content

# Copy files and directories
cp source.txt dest.txt
cp -r src/ backup/              # Copy directory recursively

# Move and rename
mv old-name.js new-name.js
mv file.txt /var/data/

# Remove files and directories
rm file.txt
rm -r directory/                # Remove directory recursively
rm -rf node_modules/            # Force remove (use with caution!)
```

## Viewing and Searching File Content

### Reading Files

```bash
# Display entire file
cat server.js

# Display with line numbers
cat -n server.js

# View large files page by page
less /var/log/syslog
# In less: / to search, n for next match, q to quit

# View first/last lines
head -n 20 server.js            # First 20 lines
tail -n 50 /var/log/app.log     # Last 50 lines
tail -f /var/log/app.log        # Follow log in real-time (crucial for debugging)
```

### Searching with grep

```bash
# Search for pattern in file
grep "error" /var/log/app.log

# Case-insensitive search
grep -i "error" /var/log/app.log

# Recursive search in directory
grep -r "TODO" src/

# Show line numbers
grep -n "function" server.js

# Show context (lines before/after match)
grep -B 3 -A 3 "Error" app.log

# Invert match (show lines NOT matching)
grep -v "DEBUG" app.log

# Count matches
grep -c "error" app.log

# Search with regex
grep -E "error|warning|critical" app.log
```

### Finding Files with find

```bash
# Find by name
find /var/log -name "*.log"

# Find by type (f=file, d=directory)
find . -type f -name "*.js"

# Find recently modified files (last 24 hours)
find . -type f -mtime -1

# Find large files (over 100MB)
find / -type f -size +100M

# Find and execute command
find . -name "*.tmp" -exec rm {} \;

# Find and delete (simpler syntax)
find . -name "node_modules" -type d -prune -exec rm -rf {} +
```

## Text Processing

### sed - Stream Editor

```bash
# Replace first occurrence on each line
sed 's/old/new/' file.txt

# Replace all occurrences
sed 's/old/new/g' file.txt

# Edit file in place
sed -i 's/localhost/0.0.0.0/g' config.js

# Delete lines matching pattern
sed '/^#/d' config.txt          # Remove comment lines

# Replace in specific line range
sed '10,20s/foo/bar/g' file.txt
```

### awk - Pattern Processing

```bash
# Print specific columns
awk '{print $1, $3}' access.log

# Filter by condition
awk '$9 == 500' access.log      # Lines where 9th field is 500

# Sum a column
awk '{sum += $5} END {print sum}' data.txt

# Custom field separator
awk -F',' '{print $2}' data.csv
```

## Pipes and Redirection

Pipes are the backbone of Linux command composition.

```
Command A в”Җв”Җ|pipe|в”Җв”Җ> Command B в”Җв”Җ|pipe|в”Җв”Җ> Command C
  stdout          stdin/stdout          stdin
```

```bash
# Pipe output of one command to another
cat access.log | grep "POST" | wc -l

# Count unique IP addresses
cat access.log | awk '{print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10

# Redirection
echo "log entry" > file.txt     # Overwrite
echo "another entry" >> file.txt # Append
command 2> errors.txt            # Redirect stderr
command > output.txt 2>&1        # Redirect both stdout and stderr
command &> all.txt               # Same as above (bash shortcut)
```

## Process Management

```bash
# List running processes
ps aux                          # All processes
ps aux | grep node              # Filter for Node.js

# Interactive process viewer
top                             # Basic
htop                            # Better (if installed)

# Find process by port
lsof -i :3000                   # What is using port 3000?
netstat -tlnp                   # All listening ports
ss -tlnp                        # Modern alternative to netstat

# Kill processes
kill PID                        # Graceful (SIGTERM)
kill -9 PID                     # Force kill (SIGKILL)
killall node                    # Kill all node processes

# Run in background
node server.js &                # Run in background
nohup node server.js &          # Survives terminal close
```

## Disk and Memory

```bash
# Disk usage
df -h                           # Filesystem usage (human-readable)
du -sh /var/log                 # Size of directory
du -sh * | sort -h              # Size of each item, sorted

# Memory usage
free -h                         # RAM usage
```

## Networking Commands

```bash
# Test connectivity
ping google.com
curl -I https://api.example.com  # HTTP headers only
curl -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"key":"value"}' http://localhost:3000/api

# DNS lookup
nslookup example.com
dig example.com

# Download files
wget https://example.com/file.tar.gz
curl -O https://example.com/file.tar.gz

# Check open ports
nc -zv localhost 5432            # Check if PostgreSQL port is open
```

## Environment Variables

```bash
# View all environment variables
env
printenv

# Set variable (current session)
export NODE_ENV=production
export PORT=3000

# Use in commands
echo $NODE_ENV

# Set for a single command
NODE_ENV=test npm test

# Persist in shell config
echo 'export NODE_ENV=production' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc                 # Reload config
```

## Archiving and Compression

```bash
# Create tar archive
tar -czf backup.tar.gz ./myproject/

# Extract tar archive
tar -xzf backup.tar.gz

# List contents without extracting
tar -tzf backup.tar.gz

# Flags: c=create, x=extract, z=gzip, f=file, v=verbose
```

## Practical Scenarios

### Analyzing Node.js Application Logs

```bash
# Find all errors in the last hour
find /var/log/myapp -name "*.log" -mmin -60 -exec grep -l "ERROR" {} \;

# Count errors by type
grep "ERROR" app.log | awk -F'ERROR: ' '{print $2}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# Watch logs in real-time, filtering for errors
tail -f /var/log/myapp/app.log | grep --line-buffered "ERROR\|FATAL"

# Find which endpoint is getting the most 500 errors
grep "500" access.log | awk '{print $7}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head
```

### Server Health Check Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
echo "=== Server Health Check ==="
echo "Uptime: $(uptime)"
echo "Disk Usage:"
df -h / | tail -1
echo "Memory:"
free -h | grep Mem
echo "Node.js Processes:"
ps aux | grep node | grep -v grep | wc -l
echo "Port 3000:"
ss -tlnp | grep 3000 || echo "Not listening"
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Running `rm -rf /` or `rm -rf *` in wrong directory** - Always double-check `pwd` before destructive commands
2. **Forgetting quotes around variables** - `rm $FILE` is dangerous if FILE contains spaces; use `rm "$FILE"`
3. **Using `kill -9` as first resort** - Always try `kill` (SIGTERM) first to allow graceful shutdown
4. **Not using `tail -f` for debugging** - Many developers `cat` entire log files instead of following them
5. **Ignoring exit codes** - Use `$?` to check if the last command succeeded (0 = success)
6.1.1.intuition: Linux commands are like a Swiss Army knife for servers. Each tool does one thing well, and by combining them with pipes (like snapping tools together), you can accomplish complex tasks that would take hundreds of lines of code in a programming language.

exercises.title: Log Analysis Challenge
exercises.description: Create a sample access log file with 100+ lines containing various HTTP status codes (200, 301, 404, 500). Write a one-liner pipeline that outputs the top 5 most frequent status codes with their counts, sorted in descending order.
exercises.hint: Use awk to extract the status code column, pipe to sort, then uniq -c, then sort -rn, then head -5.

exercises.title: Find and Clean Build Artifacts
exercises.description: Write a command that finds all node_modules directories and .next directories under your home folder, prints their sizes, and calculates the total space used. Do NOT delete them yet - just report.
exercises.hint: Use find with -type d -name 'node_modules', pipe to xargs du -sh, and use awk to sum sizes.

exercises.title: Process Detective
exercises.description: Write a shell script that takes a port number as an argument and reports: (1) whether anything is listening on that port, (2) the PID and process name, (3) how much memory that process is using.
exercises.hint: Use lsof -i :$PORT or ss -tlnp to find the process, then ps aux with the PID to get memory info.

exercises.title: Environment Setup Script
exercises.description: Write a bash script that checks if Node.js, npm, Docker, and Git are installed, prints their versions, and warns if any are missing. The script should exit with code 1 if any required tool is missing.
exercises.hint: Use 'command -v' or 'which' to check if a command exists. Use $? to check exit codes.

problems.problem: Permission denied when running a script
problems.cause: The script file does not have execute permissions.
problems.solution: Add execute permission with `chmod +x script.sh`. For directories, ensure you have execute permission to enter them (`chmod +x directory/`). Check file ownership with `ls -la`.

problems.problem: Command not found even though the program is installed
problems.cause: The program's binary is not in your PATH environment variable.
problems.solution: Find the binary location with `which program` or `find / -name program 2>/dev/null`. Add its directory to PATH: `export PATH=$PATH:/path/to/bin`. Make it permanent by adding to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc.

problems.problem: Disk space full on production server
problems.cause: Log files, Docker images, or temporary files consuming all available space.
problems.solution: Identify large files/directories with `du -sh /* | sort -h`. Common culprits: `/var/log` (rotate logs), Docker (`docker system prune`), `/tmp` (clean old files). Use `find /var/log -name '*.log' -mtime +30 -delete` to remove old logs.

6.1.2.body:
# SSH, Permissions, and File System

## Introduction

SSH (Secure Shell) is the primary way backend developers connect to remote servers. Understanding SSH, file permissions, and the Linux file system is essential for managing production servers, deploying applications, and securing your infrastructure.

## SSH Fundamentals

### How SSH Works

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                          в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   Your Mac   в”Ӯ   Encrypted Tunnel       в”Ӯ  Server      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   (client)   в”Ӯ в—„в•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв–ә в”Ӯ  (sshd)      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ   Port 22 (default)       в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  ~/.ssh/     в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ  ~/.ssh/      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  id_ed25519  в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ  authorized_  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  (private)   в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ  keys         в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                          в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

SSH uses public-key cryptography. You generate a key pair: the private key stays on your machine (never share it), and the public key goes on every server you want to access.

### Generating SSH Keys

```bash
# Generate a modern Ed25519 key (recommended)
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your.email@example.com"

# Or RSA with 4096 bits (for older systems)
ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -C "your.email@example.com"

# You'll be prompted for:
# 1. File location (default: ~/.ssh/id_ed25519)
# 2. Passphrase (strongly recommended)
```

This creates two files:
- `~/.ssh/id_ed25519` - Your private key (NEVER share this)
- `~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub` - Your public key (safe to share)

### Connecting to Servers

```bash
# Basic connection
ssh username@server-ip

# Specify port
ssh -p 2222 username@server-ip

# Specify key file
ssh -i ~/.ssh/my-server-key username@server-ip

# Execute a remote command without interactive session
ssh user@server "pm2 status"

# Copy your public key to a server
ssh-copy-id username@server-ip
# This adds your public key to the server's ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
```

### SSH Config File

Instead of remembering IPs and usernames, use `~/.ssh/config`:

```
# ~/.ssh/config

Host production
    HostName 203.0.113.50
    User deploy
    Port 22
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
    ForwardAgent yes

Host staging
    HostName 203.0.113.51
    User deploy
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

Host db-tunnel
    HostName 203.0.113.50
    User deploy
    LocalForward 5433 localhost:5432

Host *
    ServerAliveInterval 60
    ServerAliveCountMax 3
    AddKeysToAgent yes
```

Now you can simply type:

```bash
ssh production          # Instead of ssh deploy@203.0.113.50
ssh staging
ssh db-tunnel           # Opens a tunnel: local port 5433 -> remote port 5432
```

### SSH Tunneling (Port Forwarding)

SSH tunnels are extremely useful for accessing databases and services behind firewalls.

```
Local Port Forwarding:
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ      SSH Tunnel       в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ      в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  Local    в”Ӯ в•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв–ә в”Ӯ  Server   в”Ӯ в”Җв”Җв”Җв–ә в”Ӯ Database в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  :5433    в”Ӯ                      в”Ӯ  (jump)   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ  :5432   в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                      в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ      в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

```bash
# Local port forwarding: access remote PostgreSQL locally
ssh -L 5433:localhost:5432 deploy@production
# Now connect to localhost:5433 to reach the remote database

# Access a remote Redis through a jump server
ssh -L 6380:redis-server:6379 deploy@jump-server

# Dynamic SOCKS proxy (tunnel all traffic)
ssh -D 1080 deploy@production
```

### SCP and File Transfers

```bash
# Copy file to server
scp ./deploy.sh production:/home/deploy/

# Copy file from server
scp production:/var/log/app.log ./

# Copy directory recursively
scp -r ./dist/ production:/var/www/app/

# Use rsync for better transfers (resume, delta sync)
rsync -avz --progress ./dist/ production:/var/www/app/
rsync -avz --delete ./dist/ production:/var/www/app/  # Mirror exactly
```

## Linux File Permissions

### Understanding Permission Notation

Every file and directory has three permission sets:

```
-rwxr-xr-- 1 deploy www-data 4096 Jan 15 10:30 server.js
в”Ӯв”ңв”Җв”Өв”ңв”Җв”Өв”ңв”Җв”Ө   в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ      в””в”Җв”Җ Group
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ     в””в”Җв”Җ Owner
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җ Others (everyone else)
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җ Group permissions
в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җ Owner permissions
в””в”Җв”Җ File type (- = file, d = directory, l = symlink)

r = read    (4)
w = write   (2)
x = execute (1)
```

### Numeric (Octal) Permissions

```
Permission    Octal    Meaning
rwx           7        Read + Write + Execute
rw-           6        Read + Write
r-x           5        Read + Execute
r--           4        Read only
-wx           3        Write + Execute
-w-           2        Write only
--x           1        Execute only
---           0        No permissions
```

Common permission sets:

```bash
chmod 755 script.sh     # rwxr-xr-x  Owner: full, Others: read+execute
chmod 644 config.json   # rw-r--r--  Owner: read+write, Others: read
chmod 600 .env          # rw-------  Owner only (secrets!)
chmod 700 .ssh/         # rwx------  Owner only (SSH directory)
chmod 400 id_ed25519    # r--------  Read-only by owner (private key)
```

### Changing Permissions

```bash
# Numeric mode
chmod 755 deploy.sh

# Symbolic mode
chmod u+x deploy.sh      # Add execute for user (owner)
chmod g-w config.json     # Remove write for group
chmod o-rwx .env          # Remove all for others
chmod a+r README.md       # Add read for all

# Recursive
chmod -R 755 /var/www/app/
```

### File Ownership

```bash
# Change owner
chown deploy server.js
chown deploy:www-data server.js    # Change owner and group

# Change recursively
chown -R deploy:www-data /var/www/app/

# Change group only
chgrp www-data /var/www/app/
```

### Special Permissions

```bash
# Sticky bit - only owner can delete files in directory
chmod +t /tmp
chmod 1755 /shared-dir

# SUID - run file as the file owner
chmod u+s /usr/bin/program
chmod 4755 /usr/bin/program

# SGID - new files inherit directory's group
chmod g+s /var/www/shared/
chmod 2755 /var/www/shared/
```

## File System Deep Dive

### Important Directories for Backend Devs

```bash
/etc/nginx/              # Nginx configuration
/etc/systemd/system/     # systemd service files
/etc/ssl/                # SSL certificates
/var/log/                # Log files
/var/log/nginx/          # Nginx logs
/var/www/                # Web application files
/opt/                    # Third-party applications
/tmp/                    # Temporary files (cleared on reboot)
```

### Symlinks (Symbolic Links)

```bash
# Create a symbolic link
ln -s /var/www/app/current /var/www/app/releases/v2.1.0

# This is how many deployment tools work:
# /var/www/app/
# в”ңв”Җв”Җ current -> releases/v2.1.0/    (symlink)
# в”ңв”Җв”Җ releases/
# в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ v2.0.0/
# в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ v2.0.1/
# в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ v2.1.0/                    (latest)
# в””в”Җв”Җ shared/
#     в”ңв”Җв”Җ .env
#     в””в”Җв”Җ uploads/

# Remove symlink (not the target!)
rm /var/www/app/current   # or unlink
```

### Inodes and Hard Links

```bash
# View inode numbers
ls -i file.txt

# Check inode usage (running out of inodes is a real problem)
df -i

# Hard link (same inode, cannot cross filesystems)
ln original.txt hardlink.txt
```

### /proc File System

The `/proc` virtual filesystem gives real-time process and system information:

```bash
# CPU information
cat /proc/cpuinfo

# Memory information
cat /proc/meminfo

# Process information
cat /proc/1234/status    # Status of PID 1234
cat /proc/1234/cmdline   # Command that started it
ls -la /proc/1234/fd/    # Open file descriptors

# System limits
cat /proc/sys/fs/file-max             # Max open files system-wide
cat /proc/sys/net/core/somaxconn      # Max socket connections
```

## Practical Security Configuration

### Securing SSH on a Server

Edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`:

```
# Disable password authentication (use keys only)
PasswordAuthentication no
ChallengeResponseAuthentication no

# Disable root login
PermitRootLogin no

# Allow only specific users
AllowUsers deploy

# Change default port (optional, security through obscurity)
Port 2222

# Limit authentication attempts
MaxAuthTries 3
```

```bash
# Restart SSH after changes
sudo systemctl restart sshd
```

### Setting Up a Deploy User

```bash
# Create a deploy user
sudo adduser deploy

# Add to sudo group (use sparingly)
sudo usermod -aG sudo deploy

# Set up SSH access
sudo mkdir -p /home/deploy/.ssh
sudo chmod 700 /home/deploy/.ssh
# Add your public key
sudo tee /home/deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys << EOF
ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC... your.email@example.com
EOF
sudo chmod 600 /home/deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys
sudo chown -R deploy:deploy /home/deploy/.ssh
```

## Common Permission Patterns for Node.js Apps

```bash
# Application directory
sudo chown -R deploy:deploy /var/www/myapp
sudo chmod -R 755 /var/www/myapp

# Environment file (contains secrets)
chmod 600 /var/www/myapp/.env

# Log directory
sudo mkdir -p /var/log/myapp
sudo chown deploy:deploy /var/log/myapp
chmod 755 /var/log/myapp

# Upload directory (writable by app)
mkdir -p /var/www/myapp/uploads
chmod 755 /var/www/myapp/uploads

# SSL certificates
sudo chmod 600 /etc/ssl/private/server.key
sudo chmod 644 /etc/ssl/certs/server.crt
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Leaving private keys with wrong permissions** - SSH refuses to use keys with permissions too open. Always `chmod 600` private keys.
2. **Using `chmod 777` to "fix" permission issues** - This is a massive security risk. Diagnose the actual permission needed.
3. **Forgetting to `chown` after creating files as root** - Files created with `sudo` are owned by root. Your app user cannot read them.
4. **Not configuring SSH key passphrases** - If your laptop is stolen, an unprotected private key gives access to all your servers.
5. **Port forwarding without understanding** - Make sure you are not exposing internal services publicly.
6.1.2.intuition: Think of SSH like a secure, invisible tunnel between your computer and a remote server. The key pair works like a physical lock and key: the public key is the lock you install on every door (server) you want to access, and the private key is the only key that opens those locks. Permissions are like a bouncer at a club checking three lists: what the owner can do, what the group can do, and what everyone else can do.

exercises.title: SSH Config Setup
exercises.description: Create an SSH config file that defines three hosts: 'dev', 'staging', and 'production'. Each should use a different key file and user. Add a wildcard block that sets ServerAliveInterval to 60 seconds. Test that `ssh dev` would attempt the correct connection (you don't need a real server).
exercises.hint: Create ~/.ssh/config with Host blocks. Use 'ssh -v dev' to verify the config is being read correctly (it will fail to connect but show the parsed config).

exercises.title: Permission Audit Script
exercises.description: Write a bash script that audits a Node.js project directory and reports any security issues: files with 777 permissions, .env files readable by others, private keys with too-open permissions, and directories writable by others.
exercises.hint: Use find with -perm flags: find . -perm -o+w for world-writable, find . -name '.env' -perm -o+r for readable .env files.

exercises.title: SSH Tunnel for Database Access
exercises.description: Document and practice the SSH tunnel command to forward local port 5433 to a remote PostgreSQL database on port 5432. Then connect using psql through the tunnel. Write a script that establishes the tunnel in the background, runs a query, and tears it down.
exercises.hint: Use ssh -f -N -L 5433:localhost:5432 user@host to create a background tunnel. Use $! to capture the PID for later cleanup.

problems.problem: SSH connection refused or times out
problems.cause: The SSH daemon is not running, a firewall is blocking port 22, or the server IP/port is wrong.
problems.solution: Check if sshd is running: `sudo systemctl status sshd`. Check firewall: `sudo ufw status` or `sudo iptables -L`. Verify you can reach the port: `nc -zv server-ip 22`. Check security groups in cloud providers (AWS, GCP).
problems.symptoms: ssh: connect to host 203.0.113.50 port 22: Connection refused

problems.problem: Permission denied (publickey) when SSH connecting
problems.cause: Your public key is not in the server's authorized_keys, key permissions are wrong, or you are using the wrong key.
problems.solution: Verify key permissions: `chmod 700 ~/.ssh && chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519`. Use verbose mode: `ssh -vvv user@host` to see which keys are tried. Ensure your public key is in `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` on the server. Check the server's `/var/log/auth.log` for details.

problems.problem: Application cannot read/write files after deployment
problems.cause: Files were deployed as root or a different user, and the application user does not have the necessary permissions.
problems.solution: After deploying, fix ownership: `sudo chown -R deploy:deploy /var/www/app`. Ensure the upload directory is writable: `chmod 755 /var/www/app/uploads`. For log files, ensure the app user owns the log directory.

6.1.3.body:
# systemd, cron, and Process Management

## Introduction

In production, your Node.js application needs to start automatically on boot, restart if it crashes, run scheduled tasks, and be manageable by operations teams. This lesson covers the Linux tools that make this possible: systemd for service management, cron for scheduled tasks, and general process management techniques.

## systemd: The Modern Service Manager

systemd is the init system and service manager for most modern Linux distributions. It manages the lifecycle of services (daemons) on your system.

### Architecture Overview

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ              systemd (PID 1)                 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ nginx    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ node-app в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ postgres в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ .service в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ .service в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ .service в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ .timer   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ .socket  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ .target  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (cron-   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (socket  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (group   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  like)   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  activ.) в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  units)  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### Creating a Service for Your Node.js App

Create a service file at `/etc/systemd/system/myapp.service`:

```ini
[Unit]
Description=My Node.js Application
Documentation=https://github.com/yourorg/myapp
After=network.target postgresql.service
Wants=postgresql.service

[Service]
Type=simple
User=deploy
Group=deploy
WorkingDirectory=/var/www/myapp
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node dist/server.js
ExecReload=/bin/kill -HUP $MAINPID
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
StartLimitBurst=5
StartLimitIntervalSec=60

# Environment
Environment=NODE_ENV=production
Environment=PORT=3000
EnvironmentFile=/var/www/myapp/.env

# Security hardening
NoNewPrivileges=true
ProtectSystem=strict
ProtectHome=true
ReadWritePaths=/var/www/myapp/uploads /var/log/myapp
PrivateTmp=true

# Logging
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal
SyslogIdentifier=myapp

# Resource limits
LimitNOFILE=65535
MemoryMax=512M
CPUQuota=80%

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

### Managing Services

```bash
# Reload systemd after creating/modifying service files
sudo systemctl daemon-reload

# Start, stop, restart
sudo systemctl start myapp
sudo systemctl stop myapp
sudo systemctl restart myapp
sudo systemctl reload myapp     # Graceful reload (if supported)

# Enable/disable auto-start on boot
sudo systemctl enable myapp
sudo systemctl disable myapp

# Check status
sudo systemctl status myapp

# View logs
sudo journalctl -u myapp              # All logs
sudo journalctl -u myapp -f           # Follow (like tail -f)
sudo journalctl -u myapp --since today
sudo journalctl -u myapp --since "2024-01-15 10:00" --until "2024-01-15 11:00"
sudo journalctl -u myapp -n 100       # Last 100 lines
sudo journalctl -u myapp -p err       # Only errors

# List all services
systemctl list-units --type=service
systemctl list-units --type=service --state=running
```

### Service Dependencies

```ini
# Ensure PostgreSQL starts before your app
[Unit]
After=postgresql.service redis.service
Requires=postgresql.service         # Hard dependency - if PG fails, app fails
Wants=redis.service                 # Soft dependency - app starts even if Redis fails

# Start multiple instances
# myapp@.service (template)
[Service]
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node dist/server.js
Environment=PORT=%i

# Then: systemctl start myapp@3000 myapp@3001 myapp@3002
```

### Understanding Restart Policies

```ini
# Restart policies:
Restart=no                 # Never restart (default)
Restart=on-failure         # Restart on non-zero exit code (RECOMMENDED)
Restart=always             # Always restart (even on clean exit)
Restart=on-abnormal        # Restart on signal/timeout
Restart=on-abort           # Restart on signal only

# Restart timing
RestartSec=5               # Wait 5 seconds before restarting
StartLimitBurst=5          # Max 5 restarts...
StartLimitIntervalSec=60   # ...within 60 seconds, then give up
```

## cron: Scheduled Tasks

cron executes commands on a schedule. Essential for backups, cleanup tasks, report generation, and periodic data processing.

### Cron Schedule Format

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ minute (0-59)
в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ hour (0-23)
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ day of month (1-31)
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ month (1-12)
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ day of week (0-7, 0 and 7 = Sunday)
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
* * * * *  command to execute
```

### Common Schedules

```bash
# Edit crontab for current user
crontab -e

# List current user's crontab
crontab -l

# Common schedules
* * * * *     # Every minute
*/5 * * * *   # Every 5 minutes
0 * * * *     # Every hour (at minute 0)
0 0 * * *     # Every day at midnight
0 0 * * 0     # Every Sunday at midnight
0 2 * * *     # Every day at 2 AM
0 0 1 * *     # First day of every month
30 4 * * 1-5  # Weekdays at 4:30 AM
```

### Real-World Cron Examples for Node.js

```bash
# Database backup every day at 2 AM
0 2 * * * /var/www/myapp/scripts/backup-db.sh >> /var/log/myapp/backup.log 2>&1

# Clean up temp files every hour
0 * * * * find /var/www/myapp/tmp -type f -mmin +60 -delete

# Run a Node.js script to send digest emails every morning
0 8 * * * cd /var/www/myapp && /usr/bin/node scripts/send-digest.js >> /var/log/myapp/digest.log 2>&1

# Rotate application logs weekly
0 0 * * 0 /usr/sbin/logrotate /etc/logrotate.d/myapp

# Health check every 5 minutes
*/5 * * * * curl -sf http://localhost:3000/health || systemctl restart myapp

# Certificate renewal (Let's Encrypt)
0 0 1,15 * * certbot renew --quiet && systemctl reload nginx
```

### Cron Best Practices

```bash
# Always redirect output to a log file
0 2 * * * /path/to/script.sh >> /var/log/cron-job.log 2>&1

# Use full paths (cron has minimal PATH)
0 * * * * /usr/bin/node /var/www/myapp/scripts/task.js

# Set environment variables at the top of crontab
SHELL=/bin/bash
PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin
NODE_ENV=production
MAILTO=admin@example.com

# Lock file to prevent overlapping runs
*/5 * * * * flock -n /tmp/mytask.lock /var/www/myapp/scripts/long-task.sh
```

### systemd Timers (Modern Alternative to cron)

```ini
# /etc/systemd/system/myapp-backup.timer
[Unit]
Description=Daily database backup

[Timer]
OnCalendar=*-*-* 02:00:00
Persistent=true           # Run missed executions after downtime
RandomizedDelaySec=300     # Random delay up to 5 min (avoid thundering herd)

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target
```

```ini
# /etc/systemd/system/myapp-backup.service
[Unit]
Description=Database backup job

[Service]
Type=oneshot
User=deploy
ExecStart=/var/www/myapp/scripts/backup-db.sh
```

```bash
# Enable and start the timer
sudo systemctl enable myapp-backup.timer
sudo systemctl start myapp-backup.timer

# Check timer status
systemctl list-timers --all
```

## Process Management

### Understanding Signals

```
Signal     Number   Meaning                  Node.js Handler
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
SIGTERM    15       Graceful shutdown         process.on('SIGTERM', ...)
SIGINT     2        Interrupt (Ctrl+C)        process.on('SIGINT', ...)
SIGKILL    9        Force kill (uncatchable)  Cannot be handled
SIGHUP     1        Reload configuration      process.on('SIGHUP', ...)
SIGUSR2    12       User-defined              Used by nodemon, PM2
```

### Graceful Shutdown in Node.js

```javascript
// server.js - Production-ready signal handling
const server = app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`Server running on port ${PORT}`);
});

function gracefulShutdown(signal) {
  console.log(`Received ${signal}. Starting graceful shutdown...`);

  // Stop accepting new connections
  server.close(() => {
    console.log('HTTP server closed.');

    // Close database connections
    prisma.$disconnect().then(() => {
      console.log('Database disconnected.');

      // Close Redis
      redis.quit().then(() => {
        console.log('Redis disconnected.');
        process.exit(0);
      });
    });
  });

  // Force shutdown after 30 seconds
  setTimeout(() => {
    console.error('Forced shutdown after timeout');
    process.exit(1);
  }, 30000);
}

process.on('SIGTERM', () => gracefulShutdown('SIGTERM'));
process.on('SIGINT', () => gracefulShutdown('SIGINT'));

// Handle uncaught errors
process.on('uncaughtException', (err) => {
  console.error('Uncaught Exception:', err);
  gracefulShutdown('uncaughtException');
});

process.on('unhandledRejection', (reason) => {
  console.error('Unhandled Rejection:', reason);
  // Don't exit - just log. Or exit if you prefer fail-fast.
});
```

### Monitoring Processes

```bash
# Real-time process monitoring
htop

# Process tree (see parent-child relationships)
pstree -p

# Detailed process info
ps aux | grep node
ps -eo pid,ppid,%cpu,%mem,cmd | grep node

# Track system calls (debugging)
strace -p PID -e trace=network    # Network calls
strace -p PID -e trace=file       # File operations

# Monitor file descriptors
ls -la /proc/PID/fd | wc -l      # Count open file descriptors
lsof -p PID                       # List open files for process

# Monitor network connections
ss -tlnp | grep node             # TCP connections by node
ss -s                             # Summary statistics
```

### Resource Limits

```bash
# Check current limits
ulimit -a

# Increase open file limit (current session)
ulimit -n 65535

# Permanent limits in /etc/security/limits.conf
deploy  soft  nofile  65535
deploy  hard  nofile  65535

# Or in systemd service file
[Service]
LimitNOFILE=65535
LimitNPROC=4096
```

## Putting It All Together: Production Deployment

Here is a complete example of deploying a Node.js app with systemd:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# deploy.sh - Production deployment script

set -euo pipefail

APP_DIR=/var/www/myapp
DEPLOY_USER=deploy

echo "=== Deploying application ==="

# Pull latest code
cd $APP_DIR
git pull origin main

# Install dependencies
npm ci --production

# Build TypeScript
npm run build

# Run database migrations
npx prisma migrate deploy

# Restart with zero downtime
sudo systemctl reload-or-restart myapp

# Verify health
sleep 5
if curl -sf http://localhost:3000/health > /dev/null; then
    echo "Deployment successful!"
else
    echo "Health check failed! Rolling back..."
    git checkout HEAD~1
    npm ci --production
    npm run build
    sudo systemctl restart myapp
    exit 1
fi
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Not using `daemon-reload` after editing service files** - systemd caches unit files. Always run `sudo systemctl daemon-reload`.
2. **Cron jobs failing silently** - Always redirect output: `>> /var/log/job.log 2>&1`.
3. **Not handling SIGTERM in Node.js** - Your app gets 30 seconds to shut down before SIGKILL. Use that time to close connections.
4. **Running Node.js as root** - Always create a dedicated user. Use `cap_net_bind_service` if you need port 80.
5. **Overlapping cron jobs** - Use `flock` to prevent a slow job from running concurrently with itself.
6.1.3.intuition: systemd is like a super-reliable babysitter for your application. You tell it exactly how to start your app, and it watches over it 24/7 - restarting it if it crashes, starting it when the server boots, and keeping logs of everything. cron is like an alarm clock that triggers tasks on a schedule, while signals are like tapping someone on the shoulder (SIGTERM) versus pushing them out the door (SIGKILL).

exercises.title: Create a systemd Service
exercises.description: Write a complete systemd service file for a Node.js Express application. It should: run as the 'deploy' user, auto-restart on failure with a 5-second delay, load environment from a .env file, set a memory limit of 512MB, and log to journald. Enable it to start on boot.
exercises.hint: Use the [Unit], [Service], and [Install] sections. Key directives: User, WorkingDirectory, ExecStart, Restart, RestartSec, EnvironmentFile, MemoryMax.

exercises.title: Cron Job Suite
exercises.description: Write a crontab with the following jobs: (1) database backup at 2 AM daily, (2) log cleanup every Sunday, (3) health check every 5 minutes that restarts the service if it fails, (4) SSL certificate renewal on the 1st and 15th of each month. Include proper logging for each job.
exercises.hint: Use crontab -e. Remember to redirect both stdout and stderr. Use flock for the backup job to prevent overlapping runs.

exercises.title: Graceful Shutdown Implementation
exercises.description: Write a Node.js server that handles SIGTERM and SIGINT. On receiving these signals, it should: (1) stop accepting new connections, (2) wait for existing requests to complete (up to 30 seconds), (3) close database connections, and (4) exit. Test by sending signals with kill.
exercises.hint: Use server.close() to stop accepting connections. Track active connections with a counter. Use setTimeout as a safety net for forced shutdown.

problems.problem: systemd service keeps restarting in a loop then stops
problems.cause: The service failed too many times within the StartLimitIntervalSec window, so systemd stopped trying.
problems.solution: Check logs with `journalctl -u myapp -n 50`. Fix the root cause (missing env vars, wrong path, port conflict). Reset the failure counter: `sudo systemctl reset-failed myapp`. Adjust StartLimitBurst and StartLimitIntervalSec if the restarts are expected during startup.

problems.problem: Cron job works manually but not in cron
problems.cause: Cron runs with a minimal environment (limited PATH, no shell profile loaded).
problems.solution: Use full paths to all executables (`/usr/bin/node` not just `node`). Set PATH at the top of crontab. Set the working directory with `cd /path && command`. Add logging to capture errors: `>> /var/log/job.log 2>&1`.

problems.problem: Node.js app not releasing port after restart
problems.cause: The old process did not shut down gracefully, and the port is still held by a lingering connection in TIME_WAIT state.
problems.solution: Handle SIGTERM properly in your Node.js code. In your server, ensure server.close() is called. If the issue persists, check for child processes: `pgrep -P old_pid`. As a last resort, use SO_REUSEADDR (Express does this by default).

6.1.4.body:
# Shell Scripting Basics for Backend Devs

## Why Shell Scripting?

As a backend developer, you will write shell scripts for deployment automation, server setup, backup procedures, health checks, and CI/CD pipelines. While Node.js scripts can handle many tasks, shell scripts are the lingua franca of server automation -- they work everywhere, require no runtime installation, and integrate naturally with system tools.

## Shell Script Fundamentals

### Your First Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# deploy.sh - Deploy application to production
# Usage: ./deploy.sh [environment]

set -euo pipefail    # The most important line in any bash script

echo "Starting deployment..."
```

The `set -euo pipefail` flags:
- `-e`: Exit immediately on any error
- `-u`: Treat unset variables as errors
- `-o pipefail`: Pipe fails if any command in the pipeline fails

### Variables

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Variable assignment (no spaces around =)
APP_NAME="myapp"
PORT=3000
DEPLOY_DIR="/var/www/${APP_NAME}"

# Command substitution
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)
GIT_HASH=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)
NODE_VERSION=$(node --version)

# Default values
ENVIRONMENT=${1:-production}          # First arg, default "production"
LOG_LEVEL=${LOG_LEVEL:-info}          # Env var with default

# String operations
FILENAME="server.js"
echo "${FILENAME%.js}"                # Remove suffix: "server"
echo "${FILENAME##*.}"                # Get extension: "js"
echo "${APP_NAME^^}"                  # Uppercase: "MYAPP"
echo "${APP_NAME,,}"                  # Lowercase: "myapp"

# Arrays
SERVERS=("web1" "web2" "web3")
echo "${SERVERS[0]}"                  # First element
echo "${SERVERS[@]}"                  # All elements
echo "${#SERVERS[@]}"                 # Length: 3
```

### Conditionals

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# if/elif/else
if [[ "$NODE_ENV" == "production" ]]; then
    echo "Running in production"
elif [[ "$NODE_ENV" == "staging" ]]; then
    echo "Running in staging"
else
    echo "Running in development"
fi

# File tests
if [[ -f ".env" ]]; then
    echo ".env file exists"
fi

if [[ -d "node_modules" ]]; then
    echo "Dependencies installed"
fi

if [[ ! -f "dist/server.js" ]]; then
    echo "Build not found, building..."
    npm run build
fi

# Numeric comparisons
if [[ $PORT -lt 1024 ]]; then
    echo "Port requires root privileges"
fi

# String checks
if [[ -z "$DATABASE_URL" ]]; then
    echo "ERROR: DATABASE_URL is not set"
    exit 1
fi

if [[ -n "$REDIS_URL" ]]; then
    echo "Redis URL configured"
fi

# Command success/failure
if command -v docker &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Docker is installed"
else
    echo "Docker is NOT installed"
    exit 1
fi

# Combining conditions
if [[ -f ".env" ]] && [[ "$NODE_ENV" == "production" ]]; then
    source .env
fi
```

### Loops

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# For loop
SERVERS=("web1.example.com" "web2.example.com" "web3.example.com")
for server in "${SERVERS[@]}"; do
    echo "Deploying to $server..."
    ssh "deploy@${server}" "cd /var/www/app && git pull && npm ci && pm2 reload all"
done

# C-style for loop
for ((i=1; i<=5; i++)); do
    echo "Attempt $i of 5..."
    if curl -sf http://localhost:3000/health > /dev/null; then
        echo "Health check passed!"
        break
    fi
    sleep 2
done

# While loop
RETRIES=0
MAX_RETRIES=10
while [[ $RETRIES -lt $MAX_RETRIES ]]; do
    if pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432 > /dev/null 2>&1; then
        echo "PostgreSQL is ready!"
        break
    fi
    echo "Waiting for PostgreSQL... (attempt $((RETRIES+1))/$MAX_RETRIES)"
    RETRIES=$((RETRIES + 1))
    sleep 2
done

if [[ $RETRIES -eq $MAX_RETRIES ]]; then
    echo "PostgreSQL did not become ready in time"
    exit 1
fi

# Read file line by line
while IFS= read -r line; do
    echo "Processing: $line"
done < servers.txt

# Iterate over files
for file in src/**/*.ts; do
    echo "TypeScript file: $file"
done
```

### Functions

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Function definition
log() {
    local level=$1
    shift
    echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')] [$level] $*"
}

log "INFO" "Starting deployment"
log "ERROR" "Something went wrong"

# Function with return value
check_dependency() {
    local cmd=$1
    if command -v "$cmd" &> /dev/null; then
        return 0
    else
        return 1
    fi
}

if check_dependency "docker"; then
    log "INFO" "Docker found"
fi

# Function with output capture
get_free_port() {
    local port
    port=$(python3 -c 'import socket; s=socket.socket(); s.bind(("",0)); print(s.getsockname()[1]); s.close()')
    echo "$port"
}

FREE_PORT=$(get_free_port)
echo "Using port: $FREE_PORT"

# Cleanup function with trap
cleanup() {
    log "INFO" "Cleaning up temporary files..."
    rm -rf "$TMP_DIR"
}

TMP_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
trap cleanup EXIT    # Runs cleanup on script exit (even on error)
```

## Real-World Scripts

### Deployment Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Configuration
APP_DIR="/var/www/myapp"
RELEASES_DIR="$APP_DIR/releases"
CURRENT_LINK="$APP_DIR/current"
SHARED_DIR="$APP_DIR/shared"
KEEP_RELEASES=5

TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)
RELEASE_DIR="$RELEASES_DIR/$TIMESTAMP"

log() { echo "[$(date '+%H:%M:%S')] $*"; }

# Create release directory
log "Creating release $TIMESTAMP"
mkdir -p "$RELEASE_DIR"

# Clone/copy application
log "Copying application files..."
git archive HEAD | tar -x -C "$RELEASE_DIR"

# Install dependencies
log "Installing dependencies..."
cd "$RELEASE_DIR"
npm ci --production

# Build
log "Building..."
npm run build

# Link shared files
log "Linking shared resources..."
ln -sf "$SHARED_DIR/.env" "$RELEASE_DIR/.env"
ln -sf "$SHARED_DIR/uploads" "$RELEASE_DIR/uploads"

# Run migrations
log "Running database migrations..."
npx prisma migrate deploy

# Switch symlink (atomic deployment)
log "Switching to new release..."
ln -sfn "$RELEASE_DIR" "$CURRENT_LINK"

# Restart application
log "Restarting application..."
sudo systemctl restart myapp

# Health check
log "Running health check..."
sleep 3
if curl -sf http://localhost:3000/health > /dev/null; then
    log "Deployment successful!"
else
    log "Health check failed! Rolling back..."
    PREVIOUS=$(ls -1t "$RELEASES_DIR" | sed -n '2p')
    if [[ -n "$PREVIOUS" ]]; then
        ln -sfn "$RELEASES_DIR/$PREVIOUS" "$CURRENT_LINK"
        sudo systemctl restart myapp
        log "Rolled back to $PREVIOUS"
    fi
    exit 1
fi

# Clean up old releases
log "Cleaning old releases..."
cd "$RELEASES_DIR"
ls -1t | tail -n +$((KEEP_RELEASES + 1)) | xargs -r rm -rf

log "Done!"
```

### Database Backup Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Configuration
DB_NAME="myapp_production"
DB_USER="myapp"
BACKUP_DIR="/var/backups/postgresql"
S3_BUCKET="s3://myapp-backups/database"
RETENTION_DAYS=30

TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)
BACKUP_FILE="$BACKUP_DIR/${DB_NAME}_${TIMESTAMP}.sql.gz"

log() { echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')] $*"; }

# Create backup directory
mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"

# Create backup
log "Starting backup of $DB_NAME..."
pg_dump -U "$DB_USER" -h localhost "$DB_NAME" | gzip > "$BACKUP_FILE"

# Verify backup
BACKUP_SIZE=$(stat -f%z "$BACKUP_FILE" 2>/dev/null || stat -c%s "$BACKUP_FILE")
if [[ $BACKUP_SIZE -lt 1000 ]]; then
    log "ERROR: Backup file is suspiciously small ($BACKUP_SIZE bytes)"
    exit 1
fi

log "Backup created: $BACKUP_FILE ($BACKUP_SIZE bytes)"

# Upload to S3 (if aws cli is available)
if command -v aws &> /dev/null; then
    log "Uploading to S3..."
    aws s3 cp "$BACKUP_FILE" "$S3_BUCKET/"
    log "Upload complete"
fi

# Clean up old local backups
log "Removing backups older than $RETENTION_DAYS days..."
find "$BACKUP_DIR" -name "*.sql.gz" -mtime +$RETENTION_DAYS -delete

log "Backup completed successfully"
```

### Health Check Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

SERVICES=(
    "http://localhost:3000/health|Node.js API"
    "http://localhost:5432|PostgreSQL"
    "http://localhost:6379|Redis"
)

STATUS=0

check_http() {
    local url=$1
    local name=$2
    local response
    local http_code

    http_code=$(curl -sf -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" --max-time 5 "$url" 2>/dev/null) || true

    if [[ "$http_code" == "200" ]]; then
        echo "  [OK]   $name ($url)"
    else
        echo "  [FAIL] $name ($url) - HTTP $http_code"
        STATUS=1
    fi
}

check_port() {
    local host=$1
    local port=$2
    local name=$3

    if nc -z -w5 "$host" "$port" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "  [OK]   $name ($host:$port)"
    else
        echo "  [FAIL] $name ($host:$port)"
        STATUS=1
    fi
}

echo "=== Health Check Report ==="
echo "Time: $(date)"
echo ""

# Check HTTP endpoints
check_http "http://localhost:3000/health" "Node.js API"

# Check ports
check_port "localhost" 5432 "PostgreSQL"
check_port "localhost" 6379 "Redis"

# Check disk space
DISK_USAGE=$(df / | tail -1 | awk '{print $5}' | tr -d '%')
if [[ $DISK_USAGE -gt 90 ]]; then
    echo "  [WARN] Disk usage is at ${DISK_USAGE}%"
    STATUS=1
else
    echo "  [OK]   Disk usage: ${DISK_USAGE}%"
fi

# Check memory
MEM_AVAILABLE=$(free -m | awk '/Mem:/ {print $7}')
if [[ $MEM_AVAILABLE -lt 256 ]]; then
    echo "  [WARN] Low memory: ${MEM_AVAILABLE}MB available"
    STATUS=1
else
    echo "  [OK]   Memory available: ${MEM_AVAILABLE}MB"
fi

echo ""
if [[ $STATUS -eq 0 ]]; then
    echo "All checks passed!"
else
    echo "Some checks FAILED!"
fi

exit $STATUS
```

## Script Safety Patterns

### Input Validation

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Require arguments
if [[ $# -lt 1 ]]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <environment>"
    echo "Environments: staging, production"
    exit 1
fi

ENVIRONMENT=$1

# Validate input
case "$ENVIRONMENT" in
    staging|production)
        echo "Deploying to $ENVIRONMENT"
        ;;
    *)
        echo "ERROR: Invalid environment '$ENVIRONMENT'"
        echo "Must be 'staging' or 'production'"
        exit 1
        ;;
esac
```

### Confirmation Prompts

```bash
if [[ "$ENVIRONMENT" == "production" ]]; then
    read -r -p "Deploy to PRODUCTION? Type 'yes' to confirm: " confirm
    if [[ "$confirm" != "yes" ]]; then
        echo "Deployment cancelled."
        exit 0
    fi
fi
```

### Lock Files

```bash
LOCKFILE="/tmp/deploy.lock"

if [[ -f "$LOCKFILE" ]]; then
    PID=$(cat "$LOCKFILE")
    if kill -0 "$PID" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "Deployment already in progress (PID $PID)"
        exit 1
    else
        echo "Stale lock file found, removing"
        rm "$LOCKFILE"
    fi
fi

echo $$ > "$LOCKFILE"
trap 'rm -f "$LOCKFILE"' EXIT
```

## Debugging Shell Scripts

```bash
# Run with debug output
bash -x deploy.sh

# Enable debug in script
set -x          # Turn on debug
# ... commands ...
set +x          # Turn off debug

# Print commands as they execute (at top of script)
#!/bin/bash -x
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Forgetting `set -euo pipefail`** - Without this, scripts continue after errors, potentially doing damage.
2. **Not quoting variables** - `$VAR` vs `"$VAR"`. Unquoted variables break on spaces.
3. **Using `==` in `[ ]` vs `[[ ]]`** - Always use `[[ ]]` in bash; `[ ]` is POSIX but less powerful.
4. **Not using `local` in functions** - Variables leak to global scope without `local`.
5. **Assuming GNU tools on macOS** - macOS uses BSD versions of `sed`, `find`, etc. They differ from Linux. Use Homebrew's `coreutils` or write portable commands.
6.1.4.intuition: A shell script is like a recipe card for your server. Instead of manually typing commands every time you deploy, back up, or check health, you write them down once and let the script repeat the exact same steps perfectly every time. The `set -euo pipefail` at the top is like telling the chef: 'If any step fails, stop immediately and tell me -- don't try to serve a half-cooked dish.'

exercises.description: Write a bash deployment script that: (1) accepts an environment argument (staging/production), (2) validates the argument, (3) asks for confirmation if production, (4) creates a timestamped release directory, (5) runs npm ci and npm run build, (6) runs a health check, and (7) rolls back on failure.

exercises.hint: Use apt-get for package installation. Check if each tool is already installed before installing. Use adduser for user creation.

exercises.description: Write a script that rotates application logs: compress logs older than 1 day, delete compressed logs older than 30 days, and report the total space recovered. The script should use flock to prevent concurrent execution.

problems.problem: Script works when run manually but fails in cron or CI
problems.cause: The script depends on environment variables, PATH, or working directory that are set in your interactive shell but not available in cron/CI environments.
problems.solution: Always use full paths to executables. Set PATH explicitly at the top of the script. Source environment files explicitly. Use `cd` to set the working directory. Never rely on ~ (use $HOME instead).

problems.problem: Script fails with 'unexpected end of file'
problems.cause: Unclosed quotes, missing 'fi', 'done', or 'esac', or the file has Windows line endings (\r\n).
problems.solution: Check for matching if/fi, while/done, case/esac pairs. Fix line endings with `sed -i 's/\r$//' script.sh` or `dos2unix script.sh`. Use shellcheck for static analysis: `shellcheck script.sh`.

problems.problem: Variable expansion not working as expected
problems.cause: Using single quotes instead of double quotes. Single quotes prevent all variable expansion.
problems.solution: Use double quotes for strings with variables: `echo "Hello $NAME"`. Use single quotes only for literal strings. For complex cases, use ${variable} syntax for clarity.
problems.code:
# Wrong:
echo 'Hello $NAME'  # Outputs: Hello $NAME
# Right:
echo "Hello $NAME"  # Outputs: Hello World

# Dockerfile: Building Node.js Application Images

## Why Docker for Node.js?

Docker packages your application with its exact runtime environment - Node.js version, system dependencies, npm packages - into a portable image. This eliminates "works on my machine" problems and ensures identical behavior across development, CI, staging, and production.

## Docker Fundamentals

### Architecture Overview

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ              Docker Host                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Container 1в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Container 2в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Node.js APIв”Ӯ  в”Ӯ PostgreSQL в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Port: 3000 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Port: 5432 в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ                в”Ӯ                в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ      Docker Network          в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ      Docker Volumes          в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  (persistent data)           в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ        в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### Image Layers

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  COPY . .                   в”Ӯ  Layer 5 (changes often)
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ  RUN npm ci                 в”Ӯ  Layer 4 (changes with package.json)
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ  COPY package*.json ./      в”Ӯ  Layer 3
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ  WORKDIR /app               в”Ӯ  Layer 2
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ  node:20-alpine             в”Ӯ  Layer 1 (base, rarely changes)
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Each layer is cached. If a layer hasn't changed,
Docker reuses the cached version.
```

## Writing Dockerfiles for Node.js

### Basic Dockerfile

```dockerfile
# Dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine

# Create app directory
WORKDIR /app

# Copy package files first (better caching)
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./

# Install dependencies
RUN npm ci

# Copy application source
COPY . .

# Build TypeScript (if applicable)
RUN npm run build

# Expose port (documentation only)
EXPOSE 3000

# Start the application
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]
```

### Production-Optimized Dockerfile

```dockerfile
# ============================
# Stage 1: Build
# ============================
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder

WORKDIR /app

# Copy dependency files
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./

# Install ALL dependencies (including devDependencies for building)
RUN npm ci

# Copy source code
COPY tsconfig.json ./
COPY src/ ./src/
COPY prisma/ ./prisma/

# Generate Prisma client
RUN npx prisma generate

# Build TypeScript
RUN npm run build

# Remove dev dependencies
RUN npm ci --production && npm cache clean --force

# ============================
# Stage 2: Production
# ============================
FROM node:20-alpine AS production

# Add non-root user
RUN addgroup -g 1001 -S appgroup && \
    adduser -S appuser -u 1001 -G appgroup

# Install security updates
RUN apk update && apk upgrade && rm -rf /var/cache/apk/*

# Install dumb-init for proper signal handling
RUN apk add --no-cache dumb-init

WORKDIR /app

# Copy built artifacts from builder stage
COPY --from=builder --chown=appuser:appgroup /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder --chown=appuser:appgroup /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=builder --chown=appuser:appgroup /app/package.json ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=appuser:appgroup /app/prisma ./prisma

# Set environment
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV PORT=3000

# Switch to non-root user
USER appuser

# Expose port
EXPOSE 3000

# Health check
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --start-period=10s --retries=3 \
    CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1

# Use dumb-init as PID 1 for proper signal handling
ENTRYPOINT ["dumb-init", "--"]

# Start the application
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]
```

### The .dockerignore File

```
# .dockerignore
node_modules
npm-debug.log*
dist
.git
.gitignore
.env
.env.*
!.env.example
Dockerfile
docker-compose*.yml
.dockerignore
README.md
.vscode
.idea
coverage
*.test.ts
*.spec.ts
__tests__
.nyc_output
```

## Key Dockerfile Instructions

### FROM

```dockerfile
# Use specific version (always pin versions in production)
FROM node:20.11.0-alpine3.19

# Bad: uses latest, unpredictable
FROM node:latest

# Alpine is smaller (~50MB vs ~350MB for debian-based)
FROM node:20-alpine

# Slim is a middle ground
FROM node:20-slim
```

### COPY vs ADD

```dockerfile
# COPY - simple file/directory copy (preferred)
COPY package.json ./
COPY src/ ./src/

# ADD - has extra features (auto-extract archives, URLs)
ADD archive.tar.gz /app/  # Auto-extracts
# Prefer COPY unless you need ADD's features
```

### RUN - Reducing Layers

```dockerfile
# Bad: creates 3 layers
RUN apk update
RUN apk add curl
RUN rm -rf /var/cache/apk/*

# Good: single layer, smaller image
RUN apk update && \
    apk add --no-cache curl && \
    rm -rf /var/cache/apk/*
```

### ENV vs ARG

```dockerfile
# ARG - only available during build
ARG NODE_VERSION=20
FROM node:${NODE_VERSION}-alpine

ARG BUILD_DATE
LABEL build-date=$BUILD_DATE

# ENV - available during build AND in running container
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV PORT=3000
```

### ENTRYPOINT vs CMD

```dockerfile
# CMD - default command, easily overridden
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
# docker run myapp                    -> node server.js
# docker run myapp node repl          -> node repl (overridden)

# ENTRYPOINT - always runs, CMD becomes arguments
ENTRYPOINT ["node"]
CMD ["server.js"]
# docker run myapp                    -> node server.js
# docker run myapp script.js          -> node script.js

# Best practice for production:
ENTRYPOINT ["dumb-init", "--"]
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]
```

## Essential Docker Commands

```bash
# Build an image
docker build -t myapp:latest .
docker build -t myapp:1.0.0 -f Dockerfile.production .

# Run a container
docker run -d -p 3000:3000 --name myapp myapp:latest
docker run -d -p 3000:3000 --env-file .env --name myapp myapp:latest
docker run --rm -it myapp:latest sh  # Interactive shell

# Container management
docker ps                   # Running containers
docker ps -a                # All containers (including stopped)
docker logs myapp           # View logs
docker logs -f myapp        # Follow logs
docker exec -it myapp sh    # Shell into running container
docker stop myapp           # Graceful stop (SIGTERM)
docker kill myapp           # Force stop (SIGKILL)
docker rm myapp             # Remove container

# Image management
docker images               # List images
docker rmi myapp:latest     # Remove image
docker system prune -a      # Remove ALL unused images and containers

# Debugging
docker inspect myapp        # Container details
docker stats                # Resource usage
docker top myapp            # Running processes
```

## Signal Handling with dumb-init

Node.js as PID 1 in a container does not handle signals properly by default. `dumb-init` fixes this:

```
Without dumb-init:
docker stop myapp
  в””в”Җ> SIGTERM sent to PID 1 (node)
      в””в”Җ> Node.js doesn't forward to children
          в””в”Җ> After 10s, Docker sends SIGKILL (ungraceful)

With dumb-init:
docker stop myapp
  в””в”Җ> SIGTERM sent to PID 1 (dumb-init)
      в””в”Җ> dumb-init forwards SIGTERM to node (PID 2)
          в””в”Җ> Node.js runs graceful shutdown handlers
              в””в”Җ> Clean exit
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Running as root** - Always create and use a non-root user. Root in container = root on host (in many configurations).
2. **Using `npm install` instead of `npm ci`** - `npm ci` gives deterministic builds from lockfile. `npm install` may update packages.
3. **Not leveraging layer caching** - Copy package.json BEFORE copying source code. Dependencies change less often than code.
4. **Including dev dependencies in production** - Use multi-stage builds or `npm ci --production`.
5. **Not using .dockerignore** - Without it, `node_modules` and `.git` are copied into the image, bloating it.
6. **Not pinning base image versions** - `node:latest` today may be different tomorrow.
6.2.1.intuition: A Dockerfile is like a recipe for creating a sealed, portable lunchbox. You list every ingredient (dependencies) and step (build commands) needed. Multi-stage builds are like using a full kitchen to cook (build stage) but only packing the finished dish into the lunchbox (production stage) -- you do not need the oven and pots in the lunchbox.

exercises.title: Build a Production Dockerfile
exercises.description: Create a multi-stage Dockerfile for a TypeScript Express application with Prisma. The build stage should compile TypeScript and generate the Prisma client. The production stage should be based on Alpine, run as a non-root user, include a health check, and use dumb-init.
exercises.hint: Use 'FROM node:20-alpine AS builder' for the build stage and a fresh 'FROM node:20-alpine' for production. Copy only dist/, node_modules/, and prisma/ from the builder.

exercises.title: Optimize Image Size
exercises.description: Start with a basic Dockerfile that produces a ~1GB image (using node:20, copying everything, including devDependencies). Optimize it step by step: switch to Alpine, add .dockerignore, use multi-stage build, and production-only dependencies. Document the size at each step.
exercises.hint: Use 'docker images' to check sizes after each optimization. Alpine alone should cut the base by ~300MB. Multi-stage should remove build tools and devDependencies.

exercises.title: Docker Layer Caching Experiment
exercises.description: Build a Dockerfile two ways: (1) COPY everything then npm install, (2) COPY package*.json first, npm ci, then COPY the rest. Make a change to a source file and rebuild both. Measure and compare build times to demonstrate caching benefits.
exercises.hint: Use 'time docker build .' to measure. With proper caching, only the COPY . . and later layers rebuild when source changes.

problems.problem: Docker build fails with 'npm ERR! code ERESOLVE' or dependency errors
problems.cause: The package-lock.json is out of sync with package.json, or there are platform-specific dependencies (e.g., bcrypt native modules).
problems.solution: Delete package-lock.json and node_modules locally, run `npm install` to regenerate the lockfile, then rebuild. For native modules, ensure the Alpine image has build tools: `RUN apk add --no-cache python3 make g++` or use pre-built alternatives (bcryptjs instead of bcrypt).

problems.problem: Container runs but cannot connect to database
problems.cause: localhost inside the container refers to the container itself, not the host machine. The database is on the host or another container.
problems.solution: Use Docker networking. If database is in another container, use the container name as hostname. If on host, use `host.docker.internal` (Docker Desktop) or `--network host` (Linux). Best practice: use Docker Compose with a shared network.

problems.problem: SIGTERM not reaching Node.js process, container takes 10 seconds to stop
problems.cause: Node.js as PID 1 does not handle signals properly. Or CMD is in shell form (`CMD node server.js`) which wraps in /bin/sh.
problems.solution: Use exec form for CMD: `CMD ["node", "server.js"]`. Better: install and use dumb-init as ENTRYPOINT. Also ensure your Node.js code handles SIGTERM: `process.on('SIGTERM', () => server.close())`.

further_reading.title: Dive - Explore Docker Image Layers
further_reading.note: Visualize and optimize your Docker image layers
