# Docker Compose: Multi-Container Applications

## Why Docker Compose?

Real applications rarely run alone. A typical Node.js backend needs PostgreSQL, Redis, maybe Elasticsearch and a message broker. Docker Compose lets you define and run all these services together with a single YAML file and one command.

## Architecture

```
docker-compose.yml
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                                              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  api      в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  postgres в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  redis   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  :3000    в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Ӯ  :5432    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  :6379   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  Node.js  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ         app-network (bridge)         в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ pgdata       в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ redis-data   в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (volume)     в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (volume)     в”Ӯ         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ         в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

## Complete docker-compose.yml

```yaml
# docker-compose.yml
version: '3.8'

services:
  # ================================
  # Node.js API
  # ================================
  api:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile
      target: production        # Multi-stage build target
    container_name: myapp-api
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    environment:
      NODE_ENV: production
      PORT: 3000
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://myapp:secretpassword@postgres:5432/myapp_db
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379
    env_file:
      - .env                    # Additional env vars from file
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
    restart: unless-stopped
    networks:
      - app-network
    volumes:
      - uploads:/app/uploads    # Persistent uploads
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "--no-verbose", "--tries=1", "--spider", "http://localhost:3000/health"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 10s

  # ================================
  # PostgreSQL Database
  # ================================
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    container_name: myapp-postgres
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: myapp_db
      POSTGRES_USER: myapp
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secretpassword
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"             # Expose for local development tools
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./init-scripts:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d  # Auto-run SQL on first start
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U myapp -d myapp_db"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
    restart: unless-stopped
    networks:
      - app-network

  # ================================
  # Redis Cache
  # ================================
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    container_name: myapp-redis
    command: redis-server --maxmemory 128mb --maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    volumes:
      - redis-data:/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
    restart: unless-stopped
    networks:
      - app-network

  # ================================
  # Nginx Reverse Proxy
  # ================================
  nginx:
    image: nginx:1.25-alpine
    container_name: myapp-nginx
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "443:443"
    volumes:
      - ./nginx/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf:ro
      - ./nginx/conf.d:/etc/nginx/conf.d:ro
      - ./certbot/conf:/etc/letsencrypt:ro
      - ./certbot/www:/var/www/certbot:ro
    depends_on:
      - api
    restart: unless-stopped
    networks:
      - app-network

# ================================
# Volumes (persistent data)
# ================================
volumes:
  pgdata:
    driver: local
  redis-data:
    driver: local
  uploads:
    driver: local

# ================================
# Networks
# ================================
networks:
  app-network:
    driver: bridge
```

## Development vs Production Compose Files

### Base file (docker-compose.yml)

```yaml
version: '3.8'

services:
  api:
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://myapp:password@postgres:5432/myapp_db
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379
    depends_on:
      - postgres
      - redis
    networks:
      - app-network

  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: myapp_db
      POSTGRES_USER: myapp
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: password
    networks:
      - app-network

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    networks:
      - app-network

networks:
  app-network:
```

### Development overrides (docker-compose.dev.yml)

```yaml
version: '3.8'

services:
  api:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.dev
    volumes:
      - .:/app                  # Mount source for hot reload
      - /app/node_modules       # Exclude node_modules from mount
    environment:
      NODE_ENV: development
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
      - "9229:9229"            # Node.js debugger port
    command: npx nodemon --inspect=0.0.0.0:9229

  postgres:
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"            # Expose for local DB tools
    volumes:
      - pgdata-dev:/var/lib/postgresql/data

  redis:
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"            # Expose for local Redis tools

  # Additional dev services
  adminer:
    image: adminer
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"            # Database web UI
    networks:
      - app-network

  mailhog:
    image: mailhog/mailhog
    ports:
      - "1025:1025"            # SMTP
      - "8025:8025"            # Web UI
    networks:
      - app-network

volumes:
  pgdata-dev:
```

### Running with overrides

```bash
# Development (merges both files)
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.dev.yml up

# Or use the COMPOSE_FILE env var
export COMPOSE_FILE=docker-compose.yml:docker-compose.dev.yml
docker compose up

# Production
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d
```

## Docker Compose Commands

```bash
# Start all services
docker compose up                   # Foreground (see all logs)
docker compose up -d                # Detached (background)

# Build and start
docker compose up --build           # Rebuild images before starting

# Stop services
docker compose down                 # Stop and remove containers
docker compose down -v              # Also remove volumes (deletes data!)

# View logs
docker compose logs                 # All services
docker compose logs api             # Specific service
docker compose logs -f api          # Follow logs

# Service management
docker compose ps                   # List running services
docker compose exec api sh          # Shell into running service
docker compose run api npm test     # Run one-off command
docker compose restart api          # Restart a service

# Scaling
docker compose up -d --scale api=3  # Run 3 API instances

# Cleanup
docker compose down --rmi all       # Remove images too
docker system prune -a              # Nuclear option: clean everything
```

## Environment Variable Management

```yaml
services:
  api:
    # Method 1: Inline
    environment:
      NODE_ENV: production
      PORT: 3000

    # Method 2: From .env file
    env_file:
      - .env
      - .env.local             # Override with local values

    # Method 3: From host environment
    environment:
      API_KEY: ${API_KEY}      # Must be set on host
      DEBUG: ${DEBUG:-false}   # Default value if not set
```

### .env file for Compose

```bash
# .env (automatically loaded by docker compose)
COMPOSE_PROJECT_NAME=myapp
POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secretpassword
NODE_ENV=development
```

## Health Checks and Dependencies

```yaml
services:
  api:
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy    # Wait for healthy status
      redis:
        condition: service_started    # Just wait for start

  postgres:
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U myapp"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
      start_period: 30s
```

## Named Volumes vs Bind Mounts

```yaml
services:
  api:
    volumes:
      # Bind mount: host path -> container path
      - ./src:/app/src              # Development: live code reload
      - ./nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf:ro  # :ro = read-only

      # Named volume: managed by Docker
      - uploads:/app/uploads        # Persistent data
      - /app/node_modules           # Anonymous volume (exclude from bind)

volumes:
  uploads:                          # Declare named volumes
```

## Practical Patterns

### Database Initialization

```sql
-- init-scripts/01-create-extensions.sql
-- Files in /docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/ run on first start
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS "uuid-ossp";
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS "pgcrypto";
```

### Wait-for-it Pattern

```yaml
services:
  api:
    command: >
      sh -c "
        echo 'Waiting for database...' &&
        npx prisma migrate deploy &&
        echo 'Starting server...' &&
        node dist/server.js
      "
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
```

### Running Migrations

```bash
# Run migrations as a one-off command
docker compose exec api npx prisma migrate deploy

# Or as a separate service
docker compose run --rm api npx prisma migrate deploy
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Using `depends_on` without health checks** - `depends_on` alone only waits for container start, not for the service to be ready. Always add health checks.
2. **Storing secrets in docker-compose.yml** - Use `.env` files (gitignored) or Docker secrets for sensitive data.
3. **Bind-mounting node_modules** - On macOS/Windows, this destroys performance. Use a named volume for node_modules.
4. **Forgetting `docker compose down -v` deletes data** - Named volumes persist across `down` but not `down -v`.
5. **Not using `restart: unless-stopped`** - Without restart policies, containers do not restart after host reboot.
6.2.2.intuition: Docker Compose is like a conductor's sheet music for an orchestra. Each service (instrument) has its own part, but the compose file coordinates them all -- ensuring the database (rhythm section) starts before the API (melody), that they can all hear each other (networking), and that the music is preserved (volumes) even if the orchestra takes a break.

exercises.title: Full-Stack Docker Compose
exercises.description: Create a docker-compose.yml for a full-stack application with: Node.js API, PostgreSQL, Redis, and Nginx as reverse proxy. Include health checks for all services, proper dependency ordering, persistent volumes for database and uploads, and a shared network.
exercises.hint: Use depends_on with condition: service_healthy. Map Nginx port 80 to the API. Use pg_isready for Postgres health check and redis-cli ping for Redis.

exercises.title: Dev vs Prod Setup
exercises.description: Create a base docker-compose.yml and two override files: docker-compose.dev.yml (with hot reload, debug ports, adminer for DB management) and docker-compose.prod.yml (with resource limits, no exposed DB ports, production env vars). Write a Makefile with 'make dev' and 'make prod' commands.
exercises.hint: Use bind mounts in dev for hot reload. In prod, use 'deploy.resources.limits' for resource constraints. The Makefile should compose the correct override file.

exercises.title: Seed and Migrate
exercises.description: Add a migration service to your compose file that runs Prisma migrations and seeds the database on startup, then exits. The API service should wait for the migration service to complete successfully before starting.
exercises.hint: Use a separate service with 'profiles: [migrate]' or depends_on with condition: service_completed_successfully (Compose v2).

problems.problem: API container starts before database is ready, connection refused
problems.cause: depends_on without health check conditions only waits for container creation, not for the service inside to be ready.
problems.solution: Add healthcheck to the database service and use `depends_on: postgres: condition: service_healthy`. For PostgreSQL, use `test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U username"]`.

problems.problem: File changes not reflected in container during development
problems.cause: The source code is baked into the image at build time. Without bind mounts, changes require a rebuild.
problems.solution: Add a bind mount in your dev compose file: `volumes: ['.:/app', '/app/node_modules']`. The anonymous volume for node_modules prevents the host mount from overwriting container dependencies. Use nodemon or ts-node-dev for auto-restart.

problems.problem: Docker Compose extremely slow on macOS
problems.cause: File system sharing between macOS and Linux containers is slow, especially with many files (node_modules).
problems.solution: Use a named volume for node_modules instead of bind-mounting it. Enable VirtioFS in Docker Desktop settings (much faster than gRPC-FUSE). Consider using Docker's `watch` mode or mounting only `src/` instead of the entire project.

6.2.3.body:
# Multi-Stage Builds and Image Optimization

## Why Image Size Matters

Every megabyte in your Docker image impacts: pull times (especially in CI/CD), storage costs, startup speed, and attack surface. A typical unoptimized Node.js image can be 1GB+. With proper optimization, you can reduce it to under 150MB.

## Image Size Comparison

```
Image Base               Size (approximate)
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
node:20                  ~1.1 GB
node:20-slim             ~250 MB
node:20-alpine           ~140 MB
distroless (gcr.io)      ~120 MB
```

## Multi-Stage Builds In Depth

### The Problem

```dockerfile
# Single-stage: everything in one image
FROM node:20-alpine

WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN npm ci                    # Installs ALL dependencies (including dev)
RUN npm run build             # TypeScript compiler, needed only at build time

CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]

# Result: image contains TypeScript, dev tools, test frameworks, etc.
# None of these are needed at runtime!
```

### The Solution: Multi-Stage Builds

```dockerfile
# ============================
# Stage 1: Dependencies
# ============================
FROM node:20-alpine AS deps

WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci

# ============================
# Stage 2: Build
# ============================
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder

WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .

# Build TypeScript
RUN npm run build

# Generate Prisma client
RUN npx prisma generate

# ============================
# Stage 3: Production dependencies only
# ============================
FROM node:20-alpine AS prod-deps

WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --production

# ============================
# Stage 4: Production runtime
# ============================
FROM node:20-alpine AS runner

RUN addgroup -g 1001 -S nodejs && \
    adduser -S nodejs -u 1001 -G nodejs

RUN apk add --no-cache dumb-init

WORKDIR /app

# Copy ONLY what's needed to run
COPY --from=prod-deps --chown=nodejs:nodejs /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=builder --chown=nodejs:nodejs /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder --chown=nodejs:nodejs /app/prisma ./prisma
COPY --chown=nodejs:nodejs package.json ./

ENV NODE_ENV=production
USER nodejs
EXPOSE 3000

ENTRYPOINT ["dumb-init", "--"]
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]
```

### Size Reduction Visualization

```
Single Stage Build:
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ node:20-alpine base           (~140 MB) в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ ALL node_modules (dev+prod)   (~400 MB) в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ TypeScript source              (~5 MB)  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Test files                     (~2 MB)  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Build tools config             (~1 MB)  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Compiled dist/                 (~3 MB)  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Prisma engine                  (~20 MB) в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                               =========  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Total:                        ~571 MB    в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Multi-Stage Build:
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ node:20-alpine base           (~140 MB) в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Production node_modules only  (~120 MB) в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Compiled dist/                 (~3 MB)  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Prisma engine                  (~20 MB) в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ dumb-init                      (~1 MB)  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                               =========  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Total:                        ~284 MB    в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Savings: ~50% reduction
```

## Layer Caching Optimization

### Order Matters

```dockerfile
# SLOW: Any source change invalidates npm ci cache
COPY . .
RUN npm ci
RUN npm run build

# FAST: npm ci only reruns when package files change
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build
```

### Using BuildKit Cache Mounts

```dockerfile
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1

FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./

# Cache npm packages between builds
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.npm \
    npm ci

COPY . .
RUN npm run build
```

```bash
# Enable BuildKit
export DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1
docker build .

# Or use docker buildx
docker buildx build .
```

## Alpine-Specific Optimizations

### Handling Native Dependencies

```dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder

# Install build dependencies for native modules
RUN apk add --no-cache python3 make g++

WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci

# In production stage, no build tools needed
FROM node:20-alpine
# Native modules are already compiled in node_modules
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
```

### Avoiding Common Alpine Issues

```dockerfile
# Problem: some packages need glibc (Alpine uses musl)
# Solution: Install compatibility layer if needed
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat

# Problem: timezone data missing in Alpine
RUN apk add --no-cache tzdata
ENV TZ=UTC
```

## Advanced Optimization Techniques

### Removing Unnecessary Files from node_modules

```dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --production

# Remove unnecessary files from node_modules
RUN find node_modules -name "*.md" -delete && \
    find node_modules -name "*.map" -delete && \
    find node_modules -name "*.ts" -not -name "*.d.ts" -delete && \
    find node_modules -name "LICENSE*" -delete && \
    find node_modules -name "CHANGELOG*" -delete && \
    rm -rf node_modules/*/{test,tests,__tests__,spec,specs} && \
    rm -rf node_modules/*/{example,examples,doc,docs}
```

### Using node-prune

```dockerfile
RUN npm ci --production && \
    npx node-prune          # Removes test files, docs, etc. from node_modules
```

### Distroless Images

Google's distroless images contain only the runtime -- no shell, no package manager, no OS utilities. Maximum security.

```dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN npm ci && npm run build && npm ci --production

# Distroless: no shell, no package manager, minimal attack surface
FROM gcr.io/distroless/nodejs20-debian12
COPY --from=builder /app/dist /app/dist
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules /app/node_modules
COPY --from=builder /app/package.json /app/
WORKDIR /app
CMD ["dist/server.js"]
```

## Analyzing Image Layers

```bash
# See layer sizes
docker history myapp:latest

# Use dive for interactive exploration
# Install: brew install dive (macOS)
dive myapp:latest

# Output of docker history:
# IMAGE          CREATED       SIZE      COMMAND
# abc123         2 min ago     3.2MB     COPY dist/ ./dist/
# def456         2 min ago     45MB      COPY node_modules ./
# ghi789         5 min ago     0B        ENV NODE_ENV=production
# jkl012         10 min ago    142MB     /bin/sh -c #(nop) base image
```

## Building for Multiple Platforms

```bash
# Build for both AMD64 and ARM64 (Apple Silicon + Intel + AWS Graviton)
docker buildx create --name mybuilder --use

docker buildx build \
  --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
  -t myorg/myapp:latest \
  --push .
```

## CI/CD Cache Optimization

```yaml
# GitHub Actions with Docker layer caching
- name: Build and push
  uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
  with:
    context: .
    push: true
    tags: myorg/myapp:latest
    cache-from: type=gha         # GitHub Actions cache
    cache-to: type=gha,mode=max
```

## Security Scanning

```bash
# Scan image for vulnerabilities
docker scout cve myapp:latest

# Use Trivy (popular open-source scanner)
trivy image myapp:latest

# Snyk
snyk container test myapp:latest
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Not using multi-stage builds** - The single biggest optimization. Separating build from runtime can halve your image size.
2. **Installing dev dependencies in production stage** - Always use `npm ci --production` or separate dependency stages.
3. **Cache-busting with COPY . .** - Place frequently changing operations (COPY source) after rarely changing ones (npm ci).
4. **Not scanning for vulnerabilities** - Alpine images are smaller but can still have CVEs. Scan regularly.
5. **Ignoring build context size** - A large `.dockerignore`-less context slows down every build. Always check what is being sent.
6.2.3.intuition: Multi-stage builds are like building a house. You need scaffolding, cranes, and power tools during construction (build stage), but once the house is finished, you remove all the construction equipment. The final image is just the house itself (runtime), not the entire construction site. Each stage is a clean room where you pick only what you need for the next stage.

exercises.title: Size Optimization Challenge
exercises.description: Start with a Dockerfile using node:20 (Debian) with a single stage. Measure its size. Then optimize in steps: (1) switch to Alpine, (2) add .dockerignore, (3) implement multi-stage build, (4) prune node_modules. Document the size at each step and calculate total reduction.
exercises.hint: Use 'docker images --format "table {{.Repository}}\t{{.Tag}}\t{{.Size}}"' to compare. Tag each iteration differently.

exercises.title: BuildKit Cache Mounts
exercises.description: Modify your Dockerfile to use BuildKit cache mounts for npm. Benchmark build times with and without cache mounts by building, making a small change to package.json (add a comment), and rebuilding.
exercises.hint: Add '# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1' at the top. Use 'RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.npm npm ci'. Enable with DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1.

exercises.title: Security Audit
exercises.description: Build your optimized image, then scan it with 'docker scout' or Trivy. Fix any HIGH or CRITICAL vulnerabilities by updating base images or packages. Document the before/after vulnerability count.
exercises.hint: Install trivy with brew. Run 'trivy image myapp:latest'. Often, simply updating the Alpine base image or specific npm packages resolves CVEs.

problems.problem: Multi-stage build fails: file not found in COPY --from
problems.cause: The file path in the builder stage does not match what you are trying to copy, or the build step did not produce output.
problems.solution: Add a debug step in the builder stage: `RUN ls -la /app/dist/`. Ensure the build command actually produces output in the expected directory. Check that WORKDIR is set correctly in both stages.

problems.problem: Native npm packages crash in Alpine production stage
problems.cause: Native modules (bcrypt, sharp, etc.) compiled in the builder stage may link against libraries not present in the production stage.
problems.solution: Ensure the production stage has necessary runtime libraries: `RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat`. For sharp, add `vips-dev`. Better yet, use pure-JS alternatives (bcryptjs) or install native deps in the production stage too.

problems.problem: Build takes forever despite 'no changes'
problems.cause: Docker build context is too large (node_modules, .git, etc. being sent to daemon), or cache is not being utilized due to layer ordering.
problems.solution: Check .dockerignore is properly excluding node_modules and .git. Verify layer order: package.json copy and npm ci should come BEFORE source code copy. Use `docker build --progress=plain .` to see which layers are rebuilding.

# Docker Networking and Volumes

## Docker Networking

Networking is how containers communicate with each other, the host, and the outside world. Understanding Docker networking is critical for building multi-container applications.

### Network Types

```

в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ            в”Ӯ                                   в”Ӯ

```

### Bridge Networks (Default)

```bash
# Create a custom bridge network
docker network create app-network

# Run containers on the network
docker run -d --name api --network app-network myapp
docker run -d --name postgres --network app-network postgres:16

# Inside the 'api' container, connect to postgres using container name:
# postgresql://user:pass@postgres:5432/mydb
# Docker's embedded DNS resolves 'postgres' to the container's IP

# List networks
docker network ls

# Inspect a network
docker network inspect app-network

# Connect/disconnect running container
docker network connect app-network existing-container
docker network disconnect app-network existing-container
```

### Why Custom Bridge Networks?

```
Default bridge:
- Containers communicate by IP only (no DNS)
- All containers on same network (no isolation)
- Must use --link (deprecated)

Custom bridge:
- Automatic DNS resolution by container name
- Network isolation between different apps
- Better security
```

### Host Network (Linux only)

```bash
# Container shares host's network stack
docker run --network host myapp

# No port mapping needed - container uses host ports directly
# Pros: Best performance (no NAT overhead)
# Cons: Port conflicts possible, less isolation
```

### Port Mapping

```bash
# Map host port to container port
docker run -p 3000:3000 myapp          # host:container
docker run -p 8080:3000 myapp          # Different host port
docker run -p 127.0.0.1:3000:3000 myapp  # Bind to localhost only
docker run -P myapp                      # Random host port for all EXPOSE'd ports
```

### Container-to-Container Communication

```
With Docker Compose (recommended):

в”Ӯ  app-network (auto-created by compose)   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                          в”Ӯ

в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ                                  в”Ӯ

в”Ӯ       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–әв”Ӯ  redis   в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ

в”Ӯ         "redis:6379"в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ        в”Ӯ
```

```javascript
const dbConfig = {
```

## Docker Volumes

Volumes persist data beyond container lifecycles. Without volumes, all data inside a container is lost when the container is removed.

### Volume Types

```
Named Volumes:         Bind Mounts:           tmpfs:
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ          в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ          в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ Container в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ Container в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ Container в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ /data     в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ /app/src  в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ /tmp      в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ          в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ          в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
     в”Ӯ                      в”Ӯ                      в”Ӯ
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ Docker managesв”Ӯ    в”Ӯ Host filesystemв”Ӯ    в”Ӯ RAM only      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ /var/lib/     в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ ./src on host  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ (no disk)     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ docker/volumesв”Ӯ    в”Ӯ                в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ               в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Best for: data      Best for: dev code   Best for: secrets
```

### Named Volumes

```bash
# Create a volume
docker volume create pgdata

# Use in a container
docker run -v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data postgres:16

# List volumes
docker volume ls

# Inspect volume
docker volume inspect pgdata

# Remove volume
docker volume rm pgdata

# Remove all unused volumes
docker volume prune
```

### Bind Mounts

```bash
# Mount host directory into container
docker run -v $(pwd)/src:/app/src myapp

# Read-only mount
docker run -v $(pwd)/config:/app/config:ro myapp

# Mount single file
docker run -v $(pwd)/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf:ro nginx
```

### Volume Patterns in Docker Compose

```yaml
version: '3.8'

services:
  api:
    volumes:
      # Development: bind mount for hot reload
      - ./src:/app/src
      # Exclude node_modules from bind mount
      - /app/node_modules
      # Named volume for uploads
      - uploads:/app/uploads
      # Read-only config
      - ./config/production.json:/app/config/production.json:ro

  postgres:
    volumes:
      # Named volume for database data (persistent)
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      # Init scripts (bind mount, read-only)
      - ./db/init:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d:ro

  redis:
    volumes:
      - redis-data:/data

volumes:
  pgdata:
    driver: local
  redis-data:
    driver: local
  uploads:
    driver: local
```

### Backup and Restore Volumes

```bash
# Backup a named volume to a tar file
docker run --rm \
  -v pgdata:/source:ro \
  -v $(pwd):/backup \
  alpine tar czf /backup/pgdata-backup.tar.gz -C /source .

# Restore from backup
docker run --rm \
  -v pgdata:/target \
  -v $(pwd):/backup \
  alpine sh -c "cd /target && tar xzf /backup/pgdata-backup.tar.gz"
```

## Practical Networking Scenarios

### Multiple Environments on One Host

```yaml
# Project A
# docker-compose.yml
services:
  api:
    networks:
      - project-a-net
  postgres:
    networks:
      - project-a-net
networks:
  project-a-net:

# Project B (separate compose file, isolated network)
services:
  api:
    networks:
      - project-b-net
  postgres:
    networks:
      - project-b-net
networks:
  project-b-net:

# Projects are completely isolated from each other
```

### Connecting to Host Services

```yaml
services:
  api:
    # Docker Desktop (Mac/Windows):
    extra_hosts:
      - "host.docker.internal:host-gateway"
    environment:
      # Connect to PostgreSQL running on host machine
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://user:pass@host.docker.internal:5432/mydb
```

### Exposing Services Safely

```yaml
services:
  postgres:
    ports:
      # BAD: accessible from any network interface
      - "5432:5432"

      # GOOD: only accessible from localhost
      - "127.0.0.1:5432:5432"

  api:
    ports:
      # Public-facing service
      - "3000:3000"
```

## Debugging Network Issues

```bash
# Check container's network settings
docker inspect --format='{{json .NetworkSettings.Networks}}' myapp | jq

# Test connectivity between containers
docker exec api ping postgres
docker exec api nc -zv postgres 5432

# Check DNS resolution
docker exec api nslookup postgres

# View container logs for connection errors
docker logs api 2>&1 | grep -i "connect\|ECONNREFUSED\|timeout"

# List port mappings
docker port myapp

# Check which containers are on a network
docker network inspect app-network --format='{{range .Containers}}{{.Name}} {{end}}'
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Using the default bridge network** - Default bridge does not support DNS. Always create custom bridge networks.
2. **Hardcoding container IPs** - Container IPs change on restart. Always use container names for DNS resolution.
3. **Exposing database ports to 0.0.0.0** - Bind to 127.0.0.1 to prevent external access: `127.0.0.1:5432:5432`.
4. **Not understanding volume persistence** - `docker compose down` keeps volumes. `docker compose down -v` deletes them. Know the difference.
5. **Bind mounting over important container paths** - Mounting an empty host directory over `/app/node_modules` effectively deletes all modules inside the container.
6.2.4.intuition: Docker networks are like private phone lines between containers -- they can call each other by name (DNS), but outsiders cannot listen in. Volumes are like external hard drives that you plug into containers: the data on the drive persists even if you throw away the container (computer) and plug the drive into a new one.

exercises.title: Network Isolation Experiment
exercises.description: Create two separate Docker networks (frontend-net and backend-net). Run an Nginx container on frontend-net, a Node.js API on both networks, and PostgreSQL on backend-net only. Verify that Nginx can reach the API but NOT the database directly.
exercises.hint: Use docker network create for each network. Connect the API container to both networks. Use docker exec to test connectivity with ping or nc.

exercises.title: Volume Backup Pipeline
exercises.description: Create a script that backs up a PostgreSQL Docker volume to a timestamped tar.gz file, uploads it to a local backup directory, and prunes backups older than 7 days. Test it by creating data, backing up, destroying the volume, and restoring.
exercises.hint: Use a temporary Alpine container to access the volume and create the tar. For restore, create a fresh volume and extract the tar into it.

exercises.title: Debug a Networking Problem
exercises.description: Set up a Docker Compose file with an intentional networking bug (e.g., services on different networks, wrong hostname, missing port exposure). Document the symptoms and walk through the debugging process using docker inspect, docker exec, and docker logs.
exercises.hint: Common bugs to introduce: using 'localhost' instead of service name, putting services on different networks, forgetting to expose the right port.

problems.problem: Container cannot resolve hostname of another container
problems.cause: The containers are on different networks or using the default bridge network (which does not support DNS).
problems.solution: Ensure both containers are on the same custom bridge network. In Docker Compose, services on the same compose file share a default network automatically. For manual docker run, use `--network mynetwork` on both containers.

problems.problem: Data lost after docker compose down
problems.cause: You used `docker compose down -v` which removes named volumes, or you did not declare a named volume (used anonymous volume instead).
problems.solution: Always declare named volumes in the `volumes:` section of docker-compose.yml. Use `docker compose down` (without -v) to preserve data. Back up volumes regularly for critical data.

problems.problem: Port already allocated error when starting container
problems.cause: Another process (or another container) is already using the host port you are trying to map.
problems.solution: Find what is using the port: `lsof -i :3000` or `docker ps` to check running containers. Either stop the conflicting process or map to a different host port: `-p 3001:3000`.
problems.symptoms: Error: Bind for 0.0.0.0:3000 failed: port is already allocated

6.3.1.body:
# GitHub Actions: Workflows, Jobs, and Steps

## What is GitHub Actions?

GitHub Actions is a CI/CD platform built into GitHub. It lets you automate your build, test, and deployment pipeline directly from your repository. Every push, pull request, or scheduled event can trigger automated workflows.

## Architecture

