## Your First Workflow

```yaml
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI Pipeline

# When to run
on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

# Environment variables for all jobs
env:
  NODE_VERSION: '20'

jobs:
  # ================================
  # Build and Test
  # ================================
  build-and-test:
    name: Build & Test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Service containers (databases for testing)
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16-alpine
        env:
          POSTGRES_DB: test_db
          POSTGRES_USER: test_user
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test_password
        ports:
          - 5432:5432
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5

      redis:
        image: redis:7-alpine
        ports:
          - 6379:6379
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "redis-cli ping"
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5

    steps:
      # Checkout code
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Setup Node.js with caching
      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ env.NODE_VERSION }}
          cache: 'npm'

      # Install dependencies
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      # Lint
      - name: Lint code
        run: npm run lint

      # Type check
      - name: Type check
        run: npx tsc --noEmit

      # Run tests
      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test -- --coverage
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://test_user:test_password@localhost:5432/test_db
          REDIS_URL: redis://localhost:6379
          NODE_ENV: test

      # Upload coverage
      - name: Upload coverage
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: coverage-report
          path: coverage/
          retention-days: 7

      # Build
      - name: Build
        run: npm run build

      # Upload build artifact
      - name: Upload build
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: dist
          path: dist/
```

## Workflow Triggers (on:)

```yaml
on:
  # Push to specific branches
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
    paths:
      - 'src/**'               # Only when source changes
      - 'package.json'
    paths-ignore:
      - '**.md'                # Ignore docs changes
      - '.github/**'

  # Pull requests
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]

  # Scheduled (cron)
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 2 * * *'       # Daily at 2 AM UTC

  # Manual trigger
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      environment:
        description: 'Target environment'
        required: true
        default: 'staging'
        type: choice
        options:
          - staging
          - production

  # After another workflow
  workflow_run:
    workflows: ["CI Pipeline"]
    types: [completed]

  # Release published
  release:
    types: [published]
```

## Job Dependencies and Matrix

```yaml
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint

  test:
    needs: lint              # Runs after lint succeeds
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        node-version: [18, 20, 22]    # Test on multiple versions
        os: [ubuntu-latest]
      fail-fast: false        # Don't cancel other matrix jobs on failure
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test

  deploy:
    needs: [lint, test]      # Runs after BOTH succeed
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'  # Only on main branch
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy
        run: echo "Deploying..."
```

## Secrets and Environment Variables

```yaml
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production    # Links to GitHub environment
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to server
        env:
          SSH_KEY: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}
          AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
        run: |
          echo "$SSH_KEY" > key.pem
          chmod 600 key.pem
          ssh -i key.pem deploy@${{ secrets.SERVER_IP }} "cd /var/www/app && ./deploy.sh"
```

### Setting Up Secrets

1. Go to Repository Settings > Secrets and variables > Actions
2. Click "New repository secret"
3. Add name (e.g., `DATABASE_URL`) and value

### Environments with Protection Rules

1. Go to Repository Settings > Environments
2. Create environments: `staging`, `production`
3. Add protection rules: required reviewers, wait timer, branch restrictions

## Caching

```yaml
# Automatic caching with setup-node
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
  with:
    node-version: '20'
    cache: 'npm'           # Caches ~/.npm

# Manual caching (for other things)
- name: Cache Prisma engines
  uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: node_modules/.prisma
    key: prisma-${{ hashFiles('prisma/schema.prisma') }}

# Cache Docker layers
- name: Cache Docker layers
  uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: /tmp/.buildx-cache
    key: docker-${{ hashFiles('Dockerfile') }}
    restore-keys: docker-
```

## Reusable Workflows

```yaml
# .github/workflows/reusable-test.yml
name: Reusable Test Workflow

on:
  workflow_call:
    inputs:
      node-version:
        required: false
        type: string
        default: '20'
    secrets:
      DATABASE_URL:
        required: true

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ inputs.node-version }}
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}
```

```yaml
# .github/workflows/ci.yml - Calling the reusable workflow
name: CI
on: [push]

jobs:
  test:
    uses: ./.github/workflows/reusable-test.yml
    with:
      node-version: '20'
    secrets:
      DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}
```

## Composite Actions

```yaml
# .github/actions/setup-project/action.yml
name: Setup Project
description: Install Node.js and dependencies

inputs:
  node-version:
    description: 'Node.js version'
    required: false
    default: '20'

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
      with:
        node-version: ${{ inputs.node-version }}
        cache: 'npm'
    - run: npm ci
      shell: bash
    - run: npx prisma generate
      shell: bash
```

```yaml
# Usage in a workflow
steps:
  - uses: actions/checkout@v4
  - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-project
    with:
      node-version: '20'
  - run: npm test
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Not caching dependencies** - Without caching, npm ci runs on every build (~30-60 seconds). Always use setup-node's cache feature.
2. **Running everything in one job** - Split lint, test, and build into separate jobs so failures are clear and parallel execution is possible.
3. **Hardcoding secrets in workflows** - Never put secrets in YAML. Always use `${{ secrets.NAME }}`.
4. **Not using `fail-fast: false` in matrix** - By default, if one matrix job fails, all are cancelled. Set false to see all results.
5. **Ignoring workflow run time** - Free GitHub Actions minutes are limited. Optimize with caching, conditional jobs, and path filters.
6.3.1.intuition: GitHub Actions is like setting up dominoes in your repository. When someone pushes code (tips the first domino), a chain reaction begins: checkout the code, install dependencies, run tests, build, and deploy. Each domino (step) must fall successfully for the next one to trigger. If any domino fails, the chain stops and you are notified.

exercises.title: Complete CI Pipeline
exercises.description: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that: (1) triggers on push to main and PRs, (2) sets up Node.js with npm caching, (3) runs lint, type-check, and tests in parallel jobs, (4) uses PostgreSQL and Redis service containers for integration tests, (5) uploads test coverage as an artifact.
exercises.hint: Use the 'services' key for database containers. Set health checks so the job waits for services to be ready. Use 'needs' for job dependencies.

exercises.title: Matrix Testing
exercises.description: Create a workflow that tests your application across Node.js versions 18, 20, and 22 using a matrix strategy. Each should run independently (fail-fast: false). Add a summary step that reports which versions passed.
exercises.hint: Use strategy.matrix.node-version. Reference the version with ${{ matrix.node-version }}.

exercises.title: Reusable Workflow
exercises.description: Extract the test job into a reusable workflow that accepts node-version as input and DATABASE_URL as a secret. Call it from both a CI workflow and a nightly scheduled workflow.
exercises.hint: Use workflow_call trigger for the reusable workflow. Use 'uses: ./.github/workflows/reusable.yml' to call it.

problems.problem: Tests pass locally but fail in GitHub Actions
problems.cause: Environment differences: different Node.js version, missing environment variables, different OS (Ubuntu vs macOS), or timing-dependent tests.
problems.solution: Pin the Node.js version to match local. Set all required environment variables in the workflow. For timing issues, increase timeouts or use retries. Add `--verbose` flags to test commands for better debugging output.

problems.problem: Workflow runs are slow (10+ minutes)
problems.cause: No dependency caching, unnecessary steps, sequential jobs that could be parallel, or large Docker image builds.
problems.solution: Enable npm caching with setup-node. Run independent jobs in parallel. Use path filters to skip CI on doc-only changes. Cache Docker layers with actions/cache. Use the 'continue-on-error' strategy with matrix builds.

problems.problem: Service container (PostgreSQL) not ready when tests start
problems.cause: The service container started but PostgreSQL inside it is still initializing.
problems.solution: Add health check options to the service definition: `options: --health-cmd pg_isready --health-interval 10s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 5`. GitHub Actions will wait for the health check to pass before starting job steps.

6.3.2.body:
# Testing Pipelines: Lint, Type-Check, Test, Coverage

## The Quality Gate Pipeline

A testing pipeline enforces code quality at every push. It acts as an automated code reviewer that catches bugs, style issues, type errors, and regressions before they reach production.

```
Pull Request
    в”Ӯ
    в–ј
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  Lint   в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв–әв”Ӯ Type Check в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв–әв”Ӯ  Unit   в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв–әв”ӮIntegrationв”Ӯв”Җв”Җв–әв”ӮCoverageв”Ӯ
в”Ӯ ESLint  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ    tsc     в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  Tests  в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  Tests    в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ Report в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Prettierв”Ӯ   в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  Jest   в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  Jest     в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
    в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ                в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ
    в–ј              в–ј                в–ј              в–ј              в–ј
  FAIL?          FAIL?           FAIL?          FAIL?        < 80%?
  Block PR       Block PR       Block PR       Block PR      Warn
```

## Complete Testing Pipeline

```yaml
# .github/workflows/test.yml
name: Test Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

env:
  NODE_VERSION: '20'

jobs:
  # ================================
  # Stage 1: Code Quality (fast)
  # ================================
  lint:
    name: Lint & Format
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ env.NODE_VERSION }}
          cache: 'npm'

      - run: npm ci

      # ESLint
      - name: Run ESLint
        run: npx eslint . --format=json --output-file=eslint-report.json
        continue-on-error: true

      - name: Annotate ESLint results
        if: always()
        uses: ataylorme/eslint-annotate-action@v3
        with:
          report-json: eslint-report.json

      # Prettier
      - name: Check formatting
        run: npx prettier --check "src/**/*.{ts,js,json}"

  typecheck:
    name: Type Check
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ env.NODE_VERSION }}
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npx prisma generate     # Generate types
      - name: TypeScript check
        run: npx tsc --noEmit --pretty

  # ================================
  # Stage 2: Unit Tests (medium)
  # ================================
  unit-tests:
    name: Unit Tests
    needs: [lint, typecheck]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ env.NODE_VERSION }}
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npx prisma generate

      - name: Run unit tests
        run: npx jest --testPathPattern='unit' --coverage --coverageReporters=json-summary --coverageReporters=text
        env:
          NODE_ENV: test

      - name: Upload unit test coverage
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: unit-coverage
          path: coverage/

  # ================================
  # Stage 3: Integration Tests (slow)
  # ================================
  integration-tests:
    name: Integration Tests
    needs: [lint, typecheck]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16-alpine
        env:
          POSTGRES_DB: test_db
          POSTGRES_USER: test_user
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test_pass
        ports:
          - 5432:5432
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5
      redis:
        image: redis:7-alpine
        ports:
          - 6379:6379
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "redis-cli ping"
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ env.NODE_VERSION }}
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci

      - name: Setup database
        run: npx prisma migrate deploy
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://test_user:test_pass@localhost:5432/test_db

      - name: Seed test data
        run: npx prisma db seed
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://test_user:test_pass@localhost:5432/test_db

      - name: Run integration tests
        run: npx jest --testPathPattern='integration' --coverage --forceExit
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://test_user:test_pass@localhost:5432/test_db
          REDIS_URL: redis://localhost:6379
          NODE_ENV: test

  # ================================
  # Stage 4: Coverage Report
  # ================================
  coverage:
    name: Coverage Report
    needs: [unit-tests, integration-tests]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Download unit coverage
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: unit-coverage
          path: coverage/

      - name: Coverage threshold check
        run: |
          COVERAGE=$(cat coverage/coverage-summary.json | jq '.total.lines.pct')
          echo "Line coverage: ${COVERAGE}%"
          if (( $(echo "$COVERAGE < 80" | bc -l) )); then
            echo "::error::Coverage ${COVERAGE}% is below 80% threshold"
            exit 1
          fi

      - name: Comment coverage on PR
        uses: marocchino/sticky-pull-request-comment@v2
        with:
          header: coverage
          message: |
            ## Test Coverage Report
            | Metric | Coverage |
            |--------|----------|
            | Lines  | ${{ env.LINE_COV }}% |
            | Branch | ${{ env.BRANCH_COV }}% |
```

## ESLint Configuration for CI

```javascript
// eslint.config.js (flat config, ESLint 9+)
import eslint from '@eslint/js';
import tseslint from 'typescript-eslint';

export default tseslint.config(
  eslint.configs.recommended,
  ...tseslint.configs.recommended,
  {
    rules: {
      '@typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars': 'error',
      '@typescript-eslint/explicit-function-return-type': 'warn',
      '@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any': 'warn',
      'no-console': ['warn', { allow: ['warn', 'error'] }],
    },
  },
  {
    ignores: ['dist/', 'node_modules/', 'coverage/'],
  }
);
```

## Jest Configuration for CI

```javascript
// jest.config.ts
import type { Config } from 'jest';

const config: Config = {
  preset: 'ts-jest',
  testEnvironment: 'node',
  roots: ['<rootDir>/src'],
  moduleNameMapper: {
    '^@/(.*)$': '<rootDir>/src/$1',
  },
  coverageDirectory: 'coverage',
  coveragePathIgnorePatterns: [
    '/node_modules/',
    '/dist/',
    '/__tests__/',
    '/test/',
  ],
  coverageThreshold: {
    global: {
      branches: 70,
      functions: 80,
      lines: 80,
      statements: 80,
    },
  },
  projects: [
    {
      displayName: 'unit',
      testMatch: ['**/*.unit.test.ts'],
      testEnvironment: 'node',
    },
    {
      displayName: 'integration',
      testMatch: ['**/*.integration.test.ts'],
      testEnvironment: 'node',
      globalSetup: '<rootDir>/test/setup.ts',
      globalTeardown: '<rootDir>/test/teardown.ts',
    },
  ],
};

export default config;
```

## Pre-Commit Hooks with Husky + lint-staged

```json
// package.json
{
  "scripts": {
    "prepare": "husky",
    "lint": "eslint .",
    "lint:fix": "eslint . --fix",
    "format": "prettier --write 'src/**/*.{ts,js,json}'",
    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit"
  },
  "lint-staged": {
    "*.{ts,js}": [
      "eslint --fix",
      "prettier --write"
    ],
    "*.{json,md}": [
      "prettier --write"
    ]
  }
}
```

```bash
# Setup husky
npx husky init

# .husky/pre-commit
npx lint-staged

# .husky/pre-push
npm run typecheck && npm test
```

## Package Scripts Setup

```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "lint": "eslint .",
    "lint:fix": "eslint . --fix",
    "format": "prettier --write 'src/**/*.{ts,js}'",
    "format:check": "prettier --check 'src/**/*.{ts,js}'",
    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit",
    "test": "jest",
    "test:unit": "jest --testPathPattern=unit",
    "test:integration": "jest --testPathPattern=integration",
    "test:coverage": "jest --coverage",
    "test:ci": "jest --ci --coverage --forceExit",
    "build": "tsc",
    "ci": "npm run lint && npm run typecheck && npm run test:ci && npm run build"
  }
}
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Running all checks sequentially** - Lint, typecheck, and tests can run in parallel since they are independent. Use separate jobs.
2. **Not failing the pipeline on lint errors** - Lint warnings that never become errors are ignored. Set strict rules.
3. **Skipping integration tests in CI** - Unit tests alone miss real bugs. Always test with real databases in CI.
4. **Coverage thresholds that are too high or too low** - 80% line coverage is a good starting point. 100% is rarely practical or necessary.
5. **Not caching between pipeline runs** - Each run installs fresh dependencies without caching, wasting minutes.
6.3.2.intuition: A testing pipeline is like airport security. Each checkpoint catches different problems: the lint check catches formatting issues (metal detector), type checking catches type mismatches (X-ray machine), unit tests catch logic bugs (baggage screening), and integration tests ensure everything works together (the final boarding check). Only code that passes ALL checkpoints makes it to production.

exercises.title: Build a Quality Gate
exercises.description: Create a complete GitHub Actions workflow with four parallel stages: lint (ESLint + Prettier), typecheck (tsc), unit tests (Jest), and integration tests (Jest + PostgreSQL service container). Add a final coverage stage that checks for 80% minimum coverage and comments the result on the PR.
exercises.hint: Use 'needs' for stage dependencies. Run lint and typecheck in parallel, then tests after both pass. Use actions/upload-artifact to share coverage between jobs.

exercises.title: Pre-Commit Hook Setup
exercises.description: Set up Husky with lint-staged to run ESLint and Prettier on staged files before each commit, and TypeScript type-checking before each push. Verify that commits with lint errors are rejected.
exercises.hint: Use 'npx husky init' to set up. Configure lint-staged in package.json. Create .husky/pre-commit and .husky/pre-push files.

exercises.title: Coverage Trend Tracking
exercises.description: Modify the CI workflow to fail if coverage drops more than 2% compared to the main branch. Store the main branch coverage in a GitHub Actions artifact and compare it in PR builds.
exercises.hint: On main pushes, upload coverage-summary.json as an artifact. On PRs, download the main branch artifact and compare the values using jq.

problems.problem: ESLint passes locally but fails in CI
problems.cause: Different ESLint versions (local vs CI) or different configurations loaded. The CI environment may have stricter settings.
problems.solution: Pin ESLint version in package.json. Ensure .eslintrc is committed. Run `npm ci` (not `npm install`) in CI for deterministic installs. Check that .eslintignore covers generated files.

problems.problem: Integration tests flaky (pass sometimes, fail sometimes)
problems.cause: Tests depend on timing, shared database state, or ordering. Parallel test execution can cause conflicts.
problems.solution: Use unique database names per test run. Clean up data in beforeEach/afterEach. Use --runInBand for integration tests. Add --forceExit to prevent hanging. Increase timeouts for slow CI environments.

problems.problem: Coverage report shows 0% even though tests pass
problems.cause: Jest is running compiled JavaScript but coverage is configured for TypeScript source, or coverage path patterns are wrong.
problems.solution: Ensure ts-jest is configured properly. Check coveragePathIgnorePatterns is not accidentally excluding your source. Use `--collectCoverageFrom='src/**/*.ts'` explicitly.

6.3.3.body:
# Automated Deployment Strategies

## From CI to CD

Continuous Deployment (CD) extends CI by automatically deploying every change that passes tests. The deployment strategy you choose determines the risk, speed, and complexity of releases.

## Deployment Strategy Comparison

```
Strategy         Risk    Downtime    Complexity    Rollback Speed
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
Recreate         High    Yes         Low           Slow
Rolling          Medium  No          Medium        Medium
Blue/Green       Low     No          Medium        Instant
Canary           Low     No          High          Fast
Feature Flags    Low     No          High          Instant
```

### Recreate Deployment

```
Version 1 (stop all) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ downtime в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Version 2 (start all)

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯv1   в”Ӯ в”Ӯv1   в”Ӯ в”Ӯv1   в”Ӯ   в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–ә    в”Ӯv2   в”Ӯ в”Ӯv2   в”Ӯ в”Ӯv2   в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    (downtime)    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### Rolling Deployment

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯv1   в”Ӯ в”Ӯv1   в”Ӯ в”Ӯv1   в”Ӯ в”Җв–ә в”Ӯv2   в”Ӯ в”Ӯv1   в”Ӯ в”Ӯv1   в”Ӯ в”Җв–ә в”Ӯv2   в”Ӯ в”Ӯv2   в”Ӯ в”Ӯv1   в”Ӯ в”Җв–ә all v2
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### Blue/Green Deployment

```
Load Balancer
      в”Ӯ
      в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Blue (v1) в—„в”Җв”Җ current traffic
      в”Ӯ
      в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Green (v2) в—„в”Җв”Җ deploy here, test, then switch

After switch:
      в”Ӯ
      в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Blue (v1) в—„в”Җв”Җ standby (instant rollback)
      в”Ӯ
      в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Green (v2) в—„в”Җв”Җ current traffic
```

### Canary Deployment

```
Load Balancer
      в”Ӯ
      в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ 95% в”Җв”Җв–ә v1 instances (stable)
      в”Ӯ
      в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ 5%  в”Җв”Җв–ә v2 instance  (canary)
                    Monitor for errors...
                    If OK, gradually increase to 100%
```

## GitHub Actions Deployment Workflows

### Deploy to VPS/EC2 via SSH

```yaml
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    uses: ./.github/workflows/test.yml
    secrets: inherit

  deploy:
    name: Deploy to Production
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production        # Requires approval if configured

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Deploy via SSH
        uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1
        with:
          host: ${{ secrets.SERVER_HOST }}
          username: ${{ secrets.SERVER_USER }}
          key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          script: |
            cd /var/www/myapp
            git pull origin main
            npm ci --production
            npx prisma migrate deploy
            pm2 reload ecosystem.config.js --env production
```

### Deploy Docker Image

```yaml
name: Build and Deploy Docker

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

env:
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }}

jobs:
  build-and-push:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Login to Container Registry
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Build and push image
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          context: .
          push: true
          tags: |
            ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:latest
            ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ github.sha }}
          cache-from: type=gha
          cache-to: type=gha,mode=max

  deploy:
    needs: build-and-push
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to server
        uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1
        with:
          host: ${{ secrets.SERVER_HOST }}
          username: deploy
          key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          script: |
            docker pull ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ github.sha }}
            docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d --no-deps api
            docker image prune -f
```

### Deploy to AWS Lambda (Serverless)

```yaml
name: Deploy Serverless

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'

      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build

      - name: Configure AWS credentials
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          aws-access-key-id: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
          aws-secret-access-key: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Deploy with Serverless Framework
        run: npx serverless deploy --stage production
```

### Blue/Green with AWS

```yaml
name: Blue/Green Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Configure AWS
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          aws-access-key-id: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
          aws-secret-access-key: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Deploy to Green environment
        run: |
          # Build and push Docker image
          aws ecr get-login-password | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin $ECR_REGISTRY
          docker build -t $ECR_REGISTRY/myapp:${{ github.sha }} .
          docker push $ECR_REGISTRY/myapp:${{ github.sha }}

          # Update ECS service (green)
          aws ecs update-service \
            --cluster myapp-cluster \
            --service myapp-green \
            --task-definition myapp:${{ github.sha }}

          # Wait for deployment
          aws ecs wait services-stable \
            --cluster myapp-cluster \
            --services myapp-green

      - name: Health check green
        run: |
          for i in $(seq 1 10); do
            if curl -sf https://green.myapp.com/health; then
              echo "Green is healthy!"
              exit 0
            fi
            sleep 5
          done
          echo "Green health check failed"
          exit 1

      - name: Switch traffic to green
        run: |
          # Update ALB target group weights
          aws elbv2 modify-rule \
            --rule-arn ${{ secrets.ALB_RULE_ARN }} \
            --actions '[{"Type":"forward","TargetGroupArn":"'$GREEN_TG_ARN'","Weight":100}]'

      - name: Notify
        if: always()
        uses: slackapi/slack-github-action@v1
        with:
          payload: |
            {"text": "Deployment ${{ job.status }}: ${{ github.repository }}@${{ github.sha }}"}
        env:
          SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK }}
```

## Rollback Strategies

```yaml
# Manual rollback workflow
name: Rollback

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      version:
        description: 'Version to rollback to (git SHA or tag)'
        required: true

jobs:
  rollback:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - name: Rollback deployment
        uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1
        with:
          host: ${{ secrets.SERVER_HOST }}
          username: deploy
          key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          script: |
            cd /var/www/myapp
            git checkout ${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
            npm ci --production
            npx prisma migrate deploy
            pm2 reload all
```

## Database Migration Safety

```yaml
# Run migrations as a separate step with safeguards
- name: Run database migrations
  run: |
    # Backup before migration
    ssh deploy@$SERVER "pg_dump myapp > /tmp/pre-migration-backup.sql"

    # Run migration
    ssh deploy@$SERVER "cd /var/www/myapp && npx prisma migrate deploy"

    # Verify application health after migration
    sleep 10
    if ! curl -sf https://myapp.com/health; then
      echo "App unhealthy after migration, check logs!"
      exit 1
    fi
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **No rollback plan** - Every deployment should have a tested rollback procedure. Practice rollbacks regularly.
2. **Deploying database migrations and code simultaneously** - Migrate first (forward-compatible), deploy code second. This avoids a window where new code runs against old schema.
3. **No health checks after deployment** - Always verify the new version is actually working after deploy.
4. **Deploying on Fridays** - Avoid deploying before weekends or holidays when the team is unavailable to fix issues.
5. **No deployment notifications** - The team should know when deployments happen. Use Slack/Teams notifications.
6. **No approval gates for production** - Use GitHub Environments with required reviewers for production deployments.
6.3.3.intuition: Deployment strategies are like different ways to renovate a restaurant while keeping it open. Recreate deployment closes the restaurant for renovation (downtime). Rolling deployment renovates one table at a time while serving guests at the others. Blue/Green is like having two identical restaurants -- renovate one while guests eat at the other, then move everyone over. Canary deployment is like inviting a few taste-testers to the new menu before offering it to all guests.

exercises.title: Docker-Based CD Pipeline
exercises.description: Create a complete GitHub Actions workflow that: builds a Docker image, pushes it to GitHub Container Registry (ghcr.io), SSHs into a server and pulls the new image, and does a rolling restart with Docker Compose. Include health checks and Slack notification.
exercises.hint: Use docker/login-action for registry auth. Tag images with both 'latest' and the git SHA. Use 'docker compose up -d --no-deps service' for rolling restart.

exercises.title: Blue/Green Deploy Script
exercises.description: Write a deployment script that implements blue/green deployment on a single server using Docker Compose. Maintain two compose files (blue and green). The script should: deploy to the inactive environment, run health checks, switch Nginx to point to the new environment, and keep the old one as rollback.
exercises.hint: Use Nginx upstream blocks with different ports for blue (3001) and green (3002). Swap the Nginx config and reload to switch traffic.

exercises.title: Rollback Workflow
exercises.description: Create a manual GitHub Actions workflow (workflow_dispatch) that accepts a git SHA or tag and rolls back the production deployment to that version. Include database migration rollback considerations.
exercises.hint: Use workflow_dispatch with inputs for the version. SSH to the server, checkout the specific version, and restart. For database rollbacks, consider that not all migrations are reversible.

problems.problem: Deployment succeeds but application returns 502 Bad Gateway
problems.cause: The application started but crashed shortly after, or Nginx/load balancer cannot reach the new application instance.
problems.solution: Check application logs (`pm2 logs` or `docker logs`). Verify the app is listening on the correct port. Ensure health check passes. Common causes: missing environment variables, database connection failure, port mismatch between app and Nginx.

problems.problem: Database migration fails during deployment
problems.cause: The migration has a destructive change (dropping column) while old code is still running, or the migration has a syntax error.
problems.solution: Always test migrations in staging first. Use expand-contract pattern: (1) add new column, (2) deploy code that writes to both, (3) migrate data, (4) deploy code that reads from new column, (5) drop old column. Never drop columns in the same deployment that stops using them.

problems.problem: Deployment works but SSH action times out
problems.cause: The SSH connection is blocked by a firewall, security group, or the key/host configuration is wrong.
problems.solution: Verify the server's IP is correct in secrets. Check that port 22 is open in the security group/firewall. Ensure the SSH key in secrets matches the authorized_keys on the server. Test SSH access manually first.

further_reading.title: The Twelve-Factor App
further_reading.note: Methodology for building deployable SaaS applications

6.4.1.intuition: Think of AWS like renting an apartment building for your application. EC2 is the apartment itself (the server where your code runs). RDS is the building's secure storage room (managed database -- the building handles maintenance, backups, and security). S3 is like a self-storage facility across the street (virtually unlimited space for files). IAM is the key card system that controls who can access what.

exercises.title: Deploy Node.js to EC2
exercises.description: Launch a t3.micro EC2 instance, SSH into it, install Node.js and PM2, deploy a simple Express API, and configure a security group to allow HTTP traffic. Verify you can reach the API from your browser using the public IP.
exercises.hint: Use the AWS Free Tier. Create a key pair first. Install Node.js via NodeSource. Use PM2 to keep the app running. Open port 3000 in the security group.

exercises.title: S3 File Upload API
exercises.description: Build an Express endpoint that accepts file uploads via multer, stores them in S3 with a unique key, and returns the S3 URL. Implement a second endpoint that generates pre-signed URLs for direct client-to-S3 uploads (bypassing your server).
exercises.hint: Use @aws-sdk/client-s3 and @aws-sdk/s3-request-presigner. For pre-signed URLs, generate a PutObject signed URL with expiration.

exercises.title: RDS Connection Setup
exercises.description: Create a free-tier RDS PostgreSQL instance, configure it in a VPC with a private subnet, and connect to it from an EC2 instance using Prisma. Run migrations and verify the database is working.
exercises.hint: The EC2 and RDS security groups must allow traffic between them. Use SSH tunnel from your local machine to EC2 to manage the database with a GUI tool.

problems.problem: Cannot connect to RDS from EC2
problems.cause: The RDS security group does not allow inbound traffic from the EC2 security group on port 5432.
problems.solution: Edit the RDS security group to allow inbound PostgreSQL (port 5432) from the EC2 instance's security group ID. Ensure both are in the same VPC. Verify the RDS endpoint is correct (not the IP, which can change).

problems.problem: S3 upload fails with AccessDenied
problems.cause: The IAM user/role does not have permission to write to the S3 bucket, or the bucket policy denies the action.
problems.solution: Check the IAM policy attached to your EC2 role or IAM user. Ensure it has s3:PutObject permission on the correct bucket/prefix. Check the bucket policy for explicit denies. Use AWS CloudTrail to see the exact authorization failure.

problems.problem: EC2 instance runs out of memory and becomes unresponsive
problems.cause: Node.js application consuming too much memory on a small instance, or a memory leak in the application.
problems.solution: Add swap space: `dd if=/dev/zero of=/swapfile bs=1M count=2048 && mkswap /swapfile && swapon /swapfile`. Set Node.js memory limit: `--max-old-space-size=512`. Upgrade to a larger instance type. Use PM2's max_memory_restart to auto-restart on memory threshold.

6.4.2.intuition: Lambda functions are like vending machines. You do not run the factory (server) -- you just put your product (code) in the machine, and it dispenses results whenever someone presses a button (triggers an event). You pay per transaction, not for keeping the factory open 24/7. The downside is the first customer after the machine has been idle needs to wait while it warms up (cold start).

exercises.title: Build a Serverless REST API
exercises.description: Create a simple CRUD API using the Serverless Framework with Lambda and API Gateway. Implement endpoints for creating, reading, updating, and deleting items. Use DynamoDB or connect to an RDS PostgreSQL instance.
exercises.hint: Start with serverless create --template aws-nodejs-typescript. Use serverless-offline for local testing. Define each CRUD operation as a separate function.

exercises.title: Express App on Lambda
exercises.description: Take an existing Express application and deploy it to Lambda using serverless-http. Compare the local Express response times vs Lambda cold start and warm start times. Measure with curl and CloudWatch.
exercises.hint: Install serverless-http. Create a lambda.ts that wraps your Express app. Use a single catch-all route in serverless.yml.

exercises.title: Event-Driven Processing
exercises.description: Create a Lambda function that triggers on S3 uploads, processes the uploaded image (resize using sharp), and saves the result to a different S3 prefix. Use the Serverless Framework to configure the S3 event trigger.
exercises.hint: Use the s3 event in serverless.yml. The event object contains the bucket name and object key. Use sharp for image processing. Write the result to a 'thumbnails/' prefix.

problems.problem: Lambda function times out
problems.cause: The function execution exceeds the configured timeout, often due to database connection issues or slow external API calls.
problems.solution: Increase timeout in serverless.yml (max 900 seconds). Check if the Lambda can reach the database (VPC configuration, security groups). Initialize database connections outside the handler. Add logging to identify the slow operation.

problems.problem: Lambda package too large to deploy
problems.cause: node_modules includes unnecessary packages, or native modules add bulk.
problems.solution: Use a bundler like esbuild to tree-shake and bundle only used code. Move large dependencies to Lambda Layers. Exclude dev dependencies. Use serverless-esbuild plugin for automatic bundling.

problems.problem: Database connections exhausted under load
problems.cause: Each Lambda instance creates its own database connection. With 100 concurrent Lambdas, that is 100 connections.
problems.solution: Use connection pooling with RDS Proxy (AWS managed). Or use Prisma Data Proxy. Set connection limits in your Prisma schema. Consider using DynamoDB for serverless-native workloads.

further_reading.title: SST (Serverless Stack)
further_reading.note: Modern framework for serverless apps with live Lambda development

6.4.3.body:
# GCP and Heroku: Alternative Platforms

## Platform Comparison

```
Feature          AWS              GCP              Heroku
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
Complexity       High             Medium           Low
Learning curve   Steep            Moderate         Gentle
Cost at scale    Lowest           Competitive      Highest
Free tier        12 months        Always Free      Limited
Serverless       Lambda           Cloud Functions  N/A
Containers       ECS/EKS/Fargate  Cloud Run        Container Stack
PaaS             Elastic Bean.    App Engine       Heroku (native)
Best for         Enterprise       ML/Data/K8s      Prototypes/SMBs
```

## Google Cloud Platform (GCP)

### Cloud Run (Serverless Containers)

Cloud Run is one of GCP's best offerings: deploy any Docker container and it scales automatically, including to zero.

```dockerfile
# Dockerfile for Cloud Run
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine
RUN adduser -D appuser
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY package.json ./
USER appuser
# Cloud Run sets PORT environment variable
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]
```

```bash
# Deploy to Cloud Run
gcloud run deploy myapp \
  --source . \
  --region us-central1 \
  --allow-unauthenticated \
  --set-env-vars NODE_ENV=production \
  --set-env-vars DATABASE_URL="$DATABASE_URL" \
  --memory 512Mi \
  --cpu 1 \
  --min-instances 0 \
  --max-instances 10 \
  --port 3000
```

### Cloud Functions (Serverless Functions)

```javascript
// index.ts - Google Cloud Function
import { HttpFunction } from '@google-cloud/functions-framework';

export const helloWorld: HttpFunction = (req, res) => {
  const name = req.query.name || 'World';
  res.json({ message: `Hello, ${name}!` });
};
```

```bash
# Deploy Cloud Function
gcloud functions deploy helloWorld \
  --runtime nodejs20 \
  --trigger-http \
  --allow-unauthenticated \
  --region us-central1
```

### App Engine

```yaml
# app.yaml
runtime: nodejs20

env_variables:
  NODE_ENV: production

instance_class: F2

automatic_scaling:
  min_instances: 1
  max_instances: 10
  target_cpu_utilization: 0.65

handlers:
  - url: /.*
    script: auto
    secure: always
```

```bash
gcloud app deploy
```

### Cloud SQL (Managed PostgreSQL)

```bash
# Create Cloud SQL instance
gcloud sql instances create myapp-db \
  --database-version POSTGRES_16 \
  --tier db-f1-micro \
  --region us-central1

# Create database
gcloud sql databases create myapp --instance myapp-db

# Connect from Cloud Run (use Cloud SQL proxy)
gcloud run deploy myapp \
  --add-cloudsql-instances myproject:us-central1:myapp-db \
  --set-env-vars DATABASE_URL="postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/myapp?host=/cloudsql/myproject:us-central1:myapp-db"
```

## Heroku

Heroku is a PaaS (Platform as a Service) that makes deployment extremely simple. It is ideal for prototypes, MVPs, and small to medium applications.

### Heroku Deployment Flow

```
git push heroku main
       в”Ӯ
       в–ј
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ     в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ     в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ Detect       в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–әв”Ӯ Install deps  в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–әв”Ӯ Build        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ (Node.js)    в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ (npm ci)      в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ (npm build)  в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                                                   в”Ӯ
                                                   в–ј
                                            в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                                            в”Ӯ Launch       в”Ӯ
                                            в”Ӯ (npm start)  в”Ӯ
                                            в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### Project Setup for Heroku

```json
// package.json
{
  "name": "myapp",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "engines": {
    "node": "20.x"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node dist/server.js",
    "build": "tsc",
    "heroku-postbuild": "npm run build && npx prisma migrate deploy"
  }
}
```

```
# Procfile
web: node dist/server.js
worker: node dist/workers/queue.js
release: npx prisma migrate deploy
```

### Heroku CLI Commands

```bash
# Login
heroku login

# Create app
heroku create myapp-api

# Add PostgreSQL
heroku addons:create heroku-postgresql:essential-0

# Add Redis
heroku addons:create heroku-redis:mini

# Set environment variables
heroku config:set NODE_ENV=production
heroku config:set JWT_SECRET=your-secret-here

# Deploy
git push heroku main

# View logs
heroku logs --tail

# Run one-off commands
heroku run npx prisma migrate deploy
heroku run node scripts/seed.js

# Scale
heroku ps:scale web=2 worker=1

# Open app in browser
heroku open

# Restart
heroku restart
```

### Heroku with Docker

```
# heroku.yml
build:
  docker:
    web: Dockerfile
run:
  web: node dist/server.js
release:
  command:
    - npx prisma migrate deploy
```

```bash
heroku stack:set container
git push heroku main
```

## Platform Decision Matrix

```
Choose AWS when:
  - Enterprise scale requirements
  - Need specific services (Lambda, SQS, etc.)
  - Cost optimization is priority at scale
  - Team has AWS expertise

Choose GCP when:
  - Using Kubernetes (GKE is excellent)
  - Machine learning workloads
  - Cloud Run fits your use case
  - BigQuery for analytics

Choose Heroku when:
  - Speed of deployment is priority
  - Small team / solo developer
  - Prototype or MVP
  - Don't want to manage infrastructure
  - Budget is not the primary concern

Choose Vercel/Railway/Render when:
  - Modern JS/TS stack
  - Want Heroku-like simplicity
  - Better pricing than Heroku
  - GitHub-integrated deployment
```

## Modern Alternatives

### Railway

```bash
# Deploy from GitHub or CLI
railway init
railway up

# Add PostgreSQL
railway add --plugin postgresql

# Environment variables set automatically
```

### Render

```yaml
# render.yaml
services:
  - type: web
    name: myapp-api
    runtime: node
    buildCommand: npm ci && npm run build
    startCommand: node dist/server.js
    envVars:
      - key: NODE_ENV
        value: production
      - key: DATABASE_URL
        fromDatabase:
          name: myapp-db
          property: connectionString

databases:
  - name: myapp-db
    plan: free
```

### Fly.io

```bash
# Deploy globally (edge deployment)
fly launch
fly deploy

# Add PostgreSQL
fly postgres create

# Scale to multiple regions
fly regions add lhr sin
fly scale count 3
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Vendor lock-in** - Design your application with clean abstractions so you can switch platforms. Use environment variables for all configuration.
2. **Not understanding pricing** - Each platform charges differently. Heroku charges per dyno/hour, AWS per resource, GCP per invocation/second.
3. **Ignoring data residency** - Know which region your data lives in for compliance (GDPR, etc.).
4. **Over-engineering for small apps** - A simple Heroku or Railway deployment is often better than a complex AWS setup for apps with < 10,000 users.
5. **Not using managed databases** - Running your own PostgreSQL on a VM is asking for trouble. Use RDS, Cloud SQL, or Heroku Postgres.
6.4.3.intuition: Choosing a cloud platform is like choosing a restaurant for a group dinner. AWS is the massive buffet with hundreds of options but overwhelming choices. GCP is the modern fusion restaurant with some standout dishes (Cloud Run, BigQuery). Heroku is the fast-casual chain where you order at the counter and get food in minutes -- less customizable but incredibly convenient.

exercises.title: Deploy to Cloud Run
exercises.description: Containerize a Node.js Express application and deploy it to Google Cloud Run. Configure it to scale to zero when idle and up to 5 instances under load. Connect it to a Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance.
exercises.hint: Use gcloud run deploy --source . for automatic Dockerfile detection. Set --min-instances 0 and --max-instances 5. Use Cloud SQL proxy for database connection.

exercises.title: Heroku Pipeline
exercises.description: Deploy a Node.js app to Heroku with a pipeline: staging and production environments. Configure review apps for pull requests. Set up automatic deploys from the main branch to staging and manual promotion to production.
exercises.hint: Use heroku pipelines:create. Add staging and production apps. Enable review apps in the Heroku Dashboard under Pipeline settings.

exercises.title: Multi-Platform Comparison
exercises.description: Deploy the same Node.js application to AWS (EC2 or Lambda), GCP (Cloud Run), and Heroku. Document: deployment time, monthly cost estimate for 10,000 requests/day, cold start latency, and developer experience rating.
exercises.hint: Use free tiers where possible. Use curl to measure response times. Document each platform's deployment steps and count them.

problems.problem: Heroku app crashes with H10 error
problems.cause: The app is not binding to the PORT environment variable provided by Heroku (it uses a dynamic port, not 3000).
problems.solution: Use `process.env.PORT || 3000` in your app: `app.listen(process.env.PORT || 3000)`. Check `heroku logs --tail` for the actual error. Ensure your start script in package.json is correct.

problems.problem: Cloud Run request timeout (504)
problems.cause: The request took longer than the configured timeout (default 300 seconds), or the container is not responding to health checks.
problems.solution: Ensure your app listens on the PORT environment variable (Cloud Run sets this). Increase the timeout with --timeout flag. Check container logs: `gcloud run services logs read myapp`. Verify the container starts within 4 minutes.

problems.problem: Database connection fails after platform sleep/idle
problems.cause: The platform shut down the dyno/instance, and the database connection pool was lost. On restart, stale connections cause errors.
problems.solution: Configure connection pool with proper idle timeout and retry logic. Use Prisma's built-in connection management. For Heroku, connections drop after 30 minutes of inactivity -- handle reconnection gracefully.

6.5.1.intuition: PM2 is like a restaurant manager who oversees multiple chefs (Node.js processes). If one chef takes a break (crashes), the manager immediately brings in a replacement. During a menu change (code update), the manager swaps out chefs one at a time so customers (requests) are always being served. The manager also keeps track of each chef's performance and writes everything in a log book.

exercises.title: Cluster Mode Setup
exercises.description: Create an Express app that logs the worker PID for each request. Deploy it with PM2 in cluster mode with 4 instances. Send multiple requests and verify that different workers handle them (round-robin). Then perform a zero-downtime reload and confirm no requests are dropped.
exercises.hint: Use process.pid in the response. Use 'ab' (Apache Bench) or 'autocannon' to send concurrent requests during reload. Check that no 502 errors occur.

exercises.title: Ecosystem Configuration
exercises.description: Create a PM2 ecosystem.config.js for a production application with: API server (cluster mode, 4 instances), background worker (fork mode, 1 instance), and a scheduled job. Configure memory limits, log rotation, graceful shutdown, and environment-specific variables.
exercises.hint: Use exec_mode: 'cluster' for the API and 'fork' for the worker. Set max_memory_restart and cron_restart. Configure wait_ready and kill_timeout for graceful shutdown.

exercises.title: Monitoring Dashboard
exercises.description: Set up PM2 monitoring with custom metrics: request count, average response time, and error rate. Install pm2-logrotate and configure it to keep 7 days of logs with max 50MB per file.
exercises.hint: Use @pm2/io for custom metrics. Install pm2-logrotate with 'pm2 install pm2-logrotate'. Use 'pm2 monit' for the terminal dashboard.

problems.problem: PM2 cluster mode not distributing load evenly
problems.cause: Node.js cluster module uses round-robin by default on Linux, but OS-level scheduling on other platforms. Some requests may be heavier than others.
problems.solution: On Linux, round-robin is the default and works well. Ensure all workers are healthy with `pm2 list`. If one worker consistently has higher CPU, check for memory leaks or blocking operations. Use `pm2 monit` to compare workers.

problems.problem: PM2 reload causes brief 502 errors
problems.cause: The application is not signaling 'ready' to PM2, so PM2 starts routing traffic before the app is fully initialized (database connected, etc.).
problems.solution: Add `process.send('ready')` after your server starts listening and all connections are established. Set `wait_ready: true` and `listen_timeout: 10000` in ecosystem config. Implement proper SIGTERM handling for graceful shutdown.

problems.problem: PM2 processes not starting after server reboot
problems.cause: PM2 startup script was not generated or the process list was not saved.
problems.solution: Run `pm2 startup` and execute the printed sudo command. Then run `pm2 save` to persist the current process list. Verify with `pm2 list` after a reboot (or `sudo reboot` to test).

6.5.2.intuition: Nginx is like a hotel concierge standing at the front door. Guests (requests) arrive and the concierge handles simple tasks directly (serving static files, redirecting). For complex requests, the concierge directs guests to the right staff member (Node.js worker). The concierge also handles security (checking IDs/SSL), manages the queue (rate limiting), and ensures VIPs (WebSocket connections) get a dedicated line.

exercises.title: Production Nginx Config
exercises.description: Write a complete Nginx configuration for a Node.js application with: HTTPS redirect, SSL termination, gzip compression, static file serving with cache headers, reverse proxy to Node.js, WebSocket support, rate limiting on API routes, and security headers.
exercises.hint: Create separate location blocks for /static, /api, /ws, and /health. Use proxy_set_header for all necessary headers. Test with 'nginx -t'.

exercises.title: Load Balancing Setup
exercises.description: Configure Nginx to load-balance across 3 Node.js instances (ports 3000, 3001, 3002). Test with different algorithms (round-robin, least_conn, ip_hash). Use autocannon to benchmark each algorithm under load.
exercises.hint: Define an upstream block with all 3 servers. Change the algorithm and benchmark with 'autocannon -c 100 -d 10 http://localhost'. Compare requests/sec.

exercises.title: Nginx + Docker Compose
exercises.description: Add Nginx as a reverse proxy to a Docker Compose setup with 2 Node.js API instances. Configure Nginx to load-balance between them, serve health checks, and handle SSL with self-signed certificates for development.
exercises.hint: Use Docker Compose service names as hostnames in the upstream block. Mount the nginx.conf as a volume. Generate self-signed certs with openssl.

problems.problem: 502 Bad Gateway from Nginx
problems.cause: Nginx cannot connect to the upstream Node.js server. The app is not running, crashed, or is on a different port.
problems.solution: Check if Node.js is running: `pm2 status` or `curl localhost:3000`. Check Nginx error log: `tail /var/log/nginx/error.log`. Verify the proxy_pass URL matches the Node.js listening port. Check firewall rules between Nginx and Node.js.

problems.problem: WebSocket connections drop after 60 seconds
problems.cause: Nginx's default proxy_read_timeout is 60 seconds. Idle WebSocket connections are closed after this timeout.
problems.solution: Increase timeouts for WebSocket locations: `proxy_read_timeout 86400s;` (24 hours). Also set `proxy_send_timeout 86400s;`. Implement ping/pong keepalive in your WebSocket application.

problems.problem: Real client IP not available in Express (shows 127.0.0.1)
problems.cause: Express does not trust the proxy headers by default, so it ignores X-Forwarded-For and X-Real-IP.
problems.solution: Add `app.set('trust proxy', 1)` in Express. Ensure Nginx sets the headers: `proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;` and `proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;`.

further_reading.title: DigitalOcean - Nginx as Node.js Reverse Proxy
Mozilla SSL Config Generator
further_reading.note: Generate secure SSL configuration for Nginx

6.5.3.intuition: SSL/TLS is like putting your letter (HTTP request) in a tamper-proof, addressed envelope before mailing it. Without encryption, anyone who handles the mail (routers, ISPs, attackers on public WiFi) can read the contents. TLS termination at Nginx is like having a secure mailroom at the building entrance -- the mailroom opens the encrypted envelope and passes the letter internally through the secure building (private network) to the right office (Node.js).

exercises.title: HTTPS with Let's Encrypt
exercises.description: Set up HTTPS for a Node.js application using Certbot and Nginx. Configure auto-renewal, HTTP to HTTPS redirect, HSTS header, and test with SSL Labs to achieve an A+ grade.
exercises.hint: Run certbot --nginx for automatic configuration. Add HSTS header. Disable TLS 1.0/1.1. Use ssl_prefer_server_ciphers and modern cipher suites. Check with ssllabs.com.

exercises.title: Docker + HTTPS
exercises.description: Create a Docker Compose setup with Nginx handling SSL termination for a Node.js API. Use Certbot in a separate container for certificate management. Include a renewal script that runs via cron.
exercises.hint: Use shared volumes between Nginx and Certbot containers. The certbot container runs once for initial cert, then you add a cron or script to renew periodically.

exercises.title: Certificate Monitoring
exercises.description: Write a script that checks SSL certificate expiration for a list of domains and alerts (via stdout or webhook) if any certificate expires within 30 days. Set it up as a cron job that runs daily.
exercises.hint: Use openssl s_client to connect and openssl x509 to read the expiration date. Compare with current date. Loop over an array of domains.

problems.problem: Certbot fails with 'connection refused' during challenge
problems.cause: Port 80 is not open in the firewall/security group, or another service is using port 80.
problems.solution: Ensure port 80 is open: `sudo ufw allow 80`. Check if Nginx is running and listening on port 80. If using standalone mode, stop Nginx first. For AWS, check the security group allows inbound port 80.

problems.problem: Browser shows 'Your connection is not private' after setting up SSL
problems.cause: Using a self-signed certificate, certificate does not match the domain, or the certificate chain is incomplete.
problems.solution: In production, use Let's Encrypt (free and trusted). Ensure the domain in the certificate matches the URL. Use fullchain.pem (not just cert.pem) to include the full certificate chain.

problems.problem: Mixed content warnings after enabling HTTPS
problems.cause: The page is served over HTTPS but includes resources (images, scripts, API calls) loaded over HTTP.
problems.solution: Update all resource URLs to use HTTPS or protocol-relative URLs (//example.com/resource). Add Content-Security-Policy header: `upgrade-insecure-requests`. Search your codebase for 'http://' references.

further_reading.title: SSL Labs Server Test
further_reading.note: Test your server's SSL configuration and get a grade

6.6.1.body:
# Terraform Basics: Providers, Resources, State

## What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?

Infrastructure as Code means defining your servers, databases, networks, and other infrastructure in declarative configuration files instead of manually clicking through a cloud console. Terraform is the most popular IaC tool, supporting AWS, GCP, Azure, and dozens of other providers.

## Why Terraform?

```
Manual (Console/CLI):                 Terraform (IaC):
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ                 в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
Click "Create Instance"               Write .tf file
Fill in form fields                   terraform plan (preview)
Click "Launch"                        terraform apply (create)
Hope you remember settings            Config is version-controlled
No audit trail                        Full change history in git
Hard to replicate                     Exact replication every time
Hard to tear down                     terraform destroy
```

## Terraform Architecture

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

в”Ӯ                                 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Providers в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Resources в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (AWS,GCP) в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (EC2,RDS) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
           в”Ӯ
           в–ј
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ       Terraform Core            в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Plan в”Җв”Җв–ә Apply в”Җв”Җв–ә State       в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
           в”Ӯ
           в–ј
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ     Cloud Provider APIs         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  (AWS, GCP, Azure, etc.)       в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

## Getting Started

```bash
# Install Terraform
brew install terraform

# Verify
terraform version
```

### Project Structure

```
infrastructure/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ main.tf              # Main resources
в”ңв”Җв”Җ variables.tf         # Input variables
в”ңв”Җв”Җ outputs.tf           # Output values
в”ңв”Җв”Җ providers.tf         # Provider configuration
в”ңв”Җв”Җ terraform.tfvars     # Variable values (gitignored!)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ backend.tf           # State storage configuration
в””в”Җв”Җ modules/
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ vpc/
    в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ main.tf
    в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ variables.tf
    в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ outputs.tf
    в””в”Җв”Җ app/
        в”ңв”Җв”Җ main.tf
        в”ңв”Җв”Җ variables.tf
        в””в”Җв”Җ outputs.tf
```

## Core Concepts

### Providers

```hcl
# providers.tf
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5"
  
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region
  
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
      Project     = "myapp"
    }
  }
}
```

### Resources

```hcl
# main.tf

# VPC
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block           = "10.0.0.0/16"
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  enable_dns_support   = true

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-vpc"
  }
}

# Public Subnet
resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count             = 2
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = "10.0.${count.index + 1}.0/24"
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]

  map_public_ip_on_launch = true

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-public-${count.index + 1}"
  }
}

# Security Group
resource "aws_security_group" "app" {
  name_prefix = "${var.project}-app-"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id

  ingress {
    from_port   = 80
    to_port     = 80
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  ingress {
    from_port   = 443
    to_port     = 443
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  ingress {
    from_port   = 22
    to_port     = 22
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = [var.admin_cidr]
  }

  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }
}

# EC2 Instance
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type = var.instance_type
  subnet_id     = aws_subnet.public[0].id
  key_name      = var.key_pair_name

  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.app.id]

  user_data = templatefile("${path.module}/scripts/setup.sh", {
    node_version = var.node_version
    app_name     = var.project
  })

  root_block_device {
    volume_size = 20
    volume_type = "gp3"
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-server"
  }
}

# RDS PostgreSQL
resource "aws_db_instance" "postgres" {
  identifier     = "${var.project}-db"
  engine         = "postgres"
  engine_version = "16.1"
  instance_class = var.db_instance_class

  allocated_storage     = 20
  max_allocated_storage = 100
  storage_type          = "gp3"

  db_name  = var.db_name
  username = var.db_username
  password = var.db_password

  multi_az               = var.environment == "production"
  db_subnet_group_name   = aws_db_subnet_group.main.name
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.db.id]

  backup_retention_period = 7
  skip_final_snapshot     = var.environment != "production"
  deletion_protection     = var.environment == "production"

  storage_encrypted = true

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-db"
  }
}

# S3 Bucket
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "uploads" {
  bucket = "${var.project}-uploads-${var.environment}"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "uploads" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.uploads.id
  versioning_configuration {
    status = "Enabled"
  }
}
```

### Variables

```hcl
# variables.tf
variable "aws_region" {
  description = "AWS region"
  type        = string
  default     = "us-east-1"
}

variable "environment" {
  description = "Environment (dev, staging, production)"
  type        = string
  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "production"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or production."
  }
}

variable "project" {
  description = "Project name"
  type        = string
  default     = "myapp"
}

variable "instance_type" {
  description = "EC2 instance type"
  type        = string
  default     = "t3.small"
}

variable "db_password" {
  description = "Database password"
  type        = string
  sensitive   = true       # Won't show in logs
}

variable "db_instance_class" {
  description = "RDS instance class"
  type        = string
  default     = "db.t3.micro"
}
```

```hcl
# terraform.tfvars (gitignored!)
aws_region      = "us-east-1"
environment     = "production"
instance_type   = "t3.small"
db_password     = "super-secret-password"
```

### Outputs

```hcl
# outputs.tf
output "app_public_ip" {
  description = "Public IP of the application server"
  value       = aws_instance.app.public_ip
}

output "db_endpoint" {
  description = "RDS endpoint"
  value       = aws_db_instance.postgres.endpoint
}

output "s3_bucket" {
  description = "S3 bucket name"
  value       = aws_s3_bucket.uploads.id
}

output "database_url" {
  description = "Database connection URL"
  value       = "postgresql://${var.db_username}:${var.db_password}@${aws_db_instance.postgres.endpoint}/${var.db_name}"
  sensitive   = true
}
```

## Terraform State

State is how Terraform tracks which real resources correspond to your configuration.

```hcl
# backend.tf - Store state remotely (recommended)
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "myapp-terraform-state"
    key            = "production/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"     # State locking
    encrypt        = true
  }
}
```

## Terraform Commands

```bash
# Initialize (download providers, configure backend)
terraform init

# Preview changes
terraform plan

# Apply changes
terraform apply

# Apply with auto-approve (CI/CD)
terraform apply -auto-approve

# Destroy all resources
terraform destroy

# Show current state
terraform show

# List resources in state
terraform state list

# Import existing resource
terraform import aws_instance.app i-1234567890abcdef0

# Format files
terraform fmt

# Validate configuration
terraform validate
```

## Terraform Plan Output

```
$ terraform plan

Terraform will perform the following actions:

  # aws_instance.app will be created
  + resource "aws_instance" "app" {
      + ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
      + instance_type = "t3.small"
      + tags          = {
          + "Name" = "myapp-server"
        }
    }

  # aws_db_instance.postgres will be created
  + resource "aws_db_instance" "postgres" {
      + engine         = "postgres"
      + instance_class = "db.t3.micro"
    }

Plan: 2 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Storing state locally** - Local state cannot be shared across teams and is not backed up. Always use remote state (S3, GCS).
2. **Committing terraform.tfvars or .tfstate** - These contain secrets. Add to .gitignore.
3. **Not using `terraform plan` before `apply`** - Always review what will change before applying.
4. **Modifying infrastructure outside Terraform** - Manual changes create drift between state and reality. Always change through Terraform.
5. **Not using state locking** - Without DynamoDB locking, two people running `terraform apply` simultaneously can corrupt state.
6.6.1.intuition: Terraform is like architectural blueprints for your cloud infrastructure. Instead of building a house by making decisions on the spot (clicking in the AWS console), you draw detailed blueprints first (write .tf files), review them (terraform plan), then build exactly what is specified (terraform apply). If you want to renovate, you update the blueprints and Terraform figures out what needs to change. The state file is like a property deed that records exactly what was built.

exercises.title: Basic Infrastructure
exercises.description: Write Terraform configuration to create: a VPC with public and private subnets, an EC2 instance with a security group allowing HTTP and SSH, and an RDS PostgreSQL instance in the private subnet. Use variables for all configurable values.
exercises.hint: Start with the VPC, then subnets, internet gateway, route tables, security groups, and finally EC2 and RDS. Use count or for_each for multiple subnets.

exercises.title: Remote State Setup
exercises.description: Configure Terraform to store state in an S3 bucket with DynamoDB locking. Create the S3 bucket and DynamoDB table manually (or with a separate Terraform config), then configure the backend.
exercises.hint: The S3 bucket for state must be created before configuring the backend. Use 'terraform init -migrate-state' to move existing local state to the remote backend.

exercises.title: Multi-Environment Setup
exercises.description: Create a Terraform configuration that can deploy the same infrastructure to dev, staging, and production environments using different .tfvars files. Each environment should have different instance sizes and database configurations.
exercises.hint: Create dev.tfvars, staging.tfvars, and production.tfvars. Use 'terraform apply -var-file=production.tfvars'. Use conditionals for environment-specific settings (e.g., multi_az only in production).

problems.problem: Terraform destroy fails with dependency errors
problems.cause: Resources have dependencies that prevent deletion (e.g., a security group is still attached to a running instance).
problems.solution: Terraform usually handles dependency ordering. If it fails, try targeted destroy: `terraform destroy -target=aws_instance.app` first, then destroy the rest. Check for resources created outside Terraform that reference your resources.

problems.problem: State file out of sync with actual infrastructure
problems.cause: Someone modified infrastructure manually through the AWS console, creating drift.
problems.solution: Run `terraform plan` to detect drift. Use `terraform refresh` to update state with real-world data. For resources created outside Terraform, use `terraform import` to bring them under management. Going forward, enforce that all changes go through Terraform.

problems.problem: Terraform wants to destroy and recreate a resource instead of updating it
problems.cause: Some resource attributes are immutable (forces new). Changing them requires destroying and recreating the resource.
problems.solution: Check the `plan` output for 'forces replacement'. For databases, this is dangerous -- it will delete your data. Use lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to prevent accidental destruction. For immutable changes, use create_before_destroy lifecycle.

# Kubernetes Introduction: Pods, Services, Deployments

## What is Kubernetes?

Kubernetes (K8s) is a container orchestration platform that automates deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. It is the industry standard for running containers in production at scale.

## When to Use Kubernetes

```
Use Kubernetes when:                 Skip Kubernetes when:
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ                 в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
- Multiple microservices             - Single monolith
- Need auto-scaling                  - Small team (< 5 devs)
- Multi-region deployment            - Simple deployment needs
- High availability required         - Budget constraints
- Team has K8s expertise             - PM2 + Nginx is sufficient
- 10+ containers to manage           - < 5 containers
```

## Architecture Overview

в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ           в”Ӯв”Ӯ
